Original abstract: BACKGROUND: The live attenuated 17DD Yellow Fever vaccine is one of the most successful prophylactic interventions for controlling disease expansion ever designed and utilized in larger scale. However, increase on worldwide vaccine demands and manufacturing restrictions urge for more detailed dose sparing studies. The establishment of complementary biomarkers in addition to PRNT and Viremia could support a secure decision-making regarding the use of 17DD YF vaccine subdoses. The present work aimed at comparing the serum chemokine and cytokine kinetics triggered by five subdoses of 17DD YF Vaccine. METHODS: Neutralizing antibody titers, viremia, cytokines and chemokines were tested on blood samples obtained from eligible primary vaccinees. RESULTS AND DISCUSSION: The results demonstrated that a fifty-fold lower dose of 17DD-YF vaccine (587 IU) is able to trigger similar immunogenicity, as evidenced by significant titers of anti-YF PRNT. However, only subdoses as low as 3,013 IU elicit viremia kinetics with an early peak at five days after primary vaccination equivalent to the current dose (27,476 IU), while other subdoses show a distinct, lower in magnitude and later peak at day 6 post-vaccination. Although the subdose of 587 IU is able to trigger equivalent kinetics of IL-8/CXCL-8 and MCP-1/CCL-2, only the subdose of 3,013 IU is able to trigger similar kinetics of MIG/CXCL-9, pro-inflammatory (TNF, IFN-γ and IL-2) and modulatory cytokines (IL-5 and IL-10). CONCLUSIONS: The analysis of serum biomarkers IFN-γ and IL-10, in association to PRNT and viremia, support the recommendation of use of a ten-fold lower subdose (3,013 IU) of 17DD-YF vaccine.

Title: 17DD yellow fever vaccine: A double blind, randomized clinical trial of immunogenicity and safety on a dose-response study
Authors: Martins, Reinaldo M.; Maia, Maria de Lourdes S.; Farias, Roberto Henrique G.; Camacho, Luiz Antonio B.; Freire, Marcos S.; Galler, Ricardo; Yamamura, Anna Maya Yoshida; Almeida, Luiz Fernando C.; Lima, Sheila Maria B.; Nogueira, Rita Maria R.; Sá, Gloria Regina S.; Hokama, Darcy A.; de Carvalho, Ricardo; Freire, Ricardo Aguiar V.; Filho, Edson Pereira; Leal, Maria da Luz Fernandes; Homma, Akira
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): De-escalating doses including 10,447 IU, 3,013 IU, 587 IU, 158 IU, and 31 IU
Schedule / timing: Single vaccination with short-term viremia sampling and 30-day plus 10-month antibody follow-up
Primary endpoint: Seroconversion and neutralizing antibody GMT after dose reduction
Immunogenicity endpoint: Seroconversion, PRNT/GMT, persistence at 10 months
Safety endpoint: Reactogenicity diary for 10 days; clinical chemistry and adverse events
NI result: Similar immunogenicity down to 587 IU; very low doses (158 IU and 31 IU) appear below threshold
Key numerical result(s): Among 900 randomized men, doses from 27,476 IU down to 587 IU induced similar seroconversion rates and neutralizing antibody GMTs, with low reactogenicity and persistence among responders at 10 months.
Data extraction notes: Core yellow fever dose-response paper. Exact arm-specific denominators and confidence intervals need full text if used quantitatively. Sources: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23364472/ ; https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.4161/hv.22982
Screening note: Human randomized yellow fever dose-response study directly assessing reduced doses.
TL;DR / study gist: This is one of the key yellow fever dose-finding trials. In healthy adult men, doses as low as 587 IU looked similar to the standard 17DD dose in seroconversion and antibody titers, with low reactogenicity. It is foundational evidence that yellow fever vaccination can be substantially fractionated in adults without obvious loss of immunogenicity.
Original abstract: Objective: To verify if the Bio-Manguinhos 17DD yellow fever vaccine (17DD-YFV) used in lower doses is as immunogenic and safe as the current formulation. Results: Doses from 27,476 IU to 587 IU induced similar seroconversion rates and neutralizing antibodies geometric mean titers (GMTs). Immunity of those who seroconverted to YF was maintained for 10 mo. Reactogenicity was low for all groups. Methods: Young and healthy adult males (n = 900) were recruited and randomized into 6 groups, to receive de-escalating doses of 17DD-YFV, from 27,476 IU to 31 IU. Blood samples were collected before vaccination (for neutralization tests to yellow fever, serology for dengue and clinical chemistry), 3 to 7 d after vaccination (for viremia and clinical chemistry) and 30 d after vaccination (for new yellow fever serology and clinical chemistry). Adverse events diaries were filled out by volunteers during 10 d after vaccination. Volunteers were retested for yellow fever and dengue antibodies 10 mo later. Seropositivity for dengue was found in 87.6% of volunteers before vaccination, but this had no significant influence on conclusions. Conclusion: In young healthy adults Bio-Manguinhos/Fiocruz yellow fever vaccine can be used in much lower doses than usual. International Register ISRCTN 38082350.

Title: A pilot randomized study to assess immunogenicity, reactogenicity, safety and tolerability of two human papillomavirus vaccines administered intramuscularly and intradermally to females aged 18-26 years
Authors: Nelson, E. Anthony S.; Lam, Hugh Simon; Choi, Kai C.; Ho, Wendy C. S.; Fung, L. W. Eva; Cheng, Frankie W. T.; Sung, Rita Y. T.; Royals, Michael; Chan, Paul K. S.
Vaccine / intervention: Cervarix and Gardasil HPV vaccines, intramuscular and intradermal fractional dosing
Route of administration: Intradermal vs intramuscular; needle/syringe or PharmaJet device
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): Intradermal full-dose and 20% dose arms by different methods
Schedule / timing: Three-dose schedule at 0, 2, and 6 months; interim immunogenicity reported by day 95
Primary endpoint: Immunogenicity and tolerability across routes/doses
Immunogenicity endpoint: Seroconversion (titre ≥1:320) by day 95
Safety endpoint: Reactogenicity, safety, and tolerability
NI result: Not formally tested; all subjects seroconverted
Key numerical result(s): Forty women completed the study, and all seroconverted by day 95. Intradermal vaccination raised no major safety concerns but was more reactogenic than intramuscular administration.
Reason not meta-analyzable: Small pilot with mixed products and administration methods; limited quantitative detail in abstract.
Data extraction notes: Interesting proof-of-concept for intradermal HPV dose-sparing, but small and exploratory. Sources: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23770335/ ; https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0264410X13008128
Screening note: Pilot human study explicitly testing reduced (20%) intradermal HPV vaccine dosing.
TL;DR / study gist: This small pilot suggests HPV vaccines can be given intradermally, even at reduced doses, without major safety concerns. All women seroconverted, but intradermal delivery was more reactogenic and the trial was too small to support strong efficacy claims. It is better viewed as early proof-of-concept than definitive dose-sparing evidence.
Original abstract: Intradermal administration of human papillomavirus (HPV) vaccines could be dose-sparing and cost-saving. This pilot randomized study assessed Cervarix(®) and Gardasil(®) administered either intramuscularly or intradermally, in different doses (full-dose or reduced to 20%) by different methods (needle and syringe or PharmaJet needle-free jet injection device). Following an initial reactogenicity study of 10 male subjects, sexually naïve women aged 18-26 years were randomized to the eight study groups to receive vaccine at 0, 2 and 6 months. 42 female subjects were enrolled and complete data were available for 40 subjects. Intradermal administration of either vaccine raised no safety concerns but was more reactogenic than intramuscular administration, although still tolerable. All subjects demonstrated a seroconversion (titre≥1:320) by Day 95. Further evaluation of intradermal HPV vaccination and its potential for cost reduction in resource poor settings is warranted.

Title: Intradermal fractional booster dose of inactivated poliomyelitis vaccine with a jet injector in healthy adults
Authors: Soonawala, Darius; Verdijk, Pauline; Wijmenga-Monsuur, Alienke J.; Boog, Claire J.; Koedam, Patrick; Visser, Leo G.; Rots, Nynke Y.
Vaccine / intervention: Netherlands Vaccine Institute IPV booster, full-dose and fractional-dose via needle or jet injector
Special population: Healthy Dutch adults with documented vaccination history
Route of administration: Intradermal jet injector vs intramuscular needle/jet injector
Comparator (full dose / standard / other: Full-dose IM IPV by needle (reference) and full-dose IM jet injector
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): 1/5 dose IM by needle; 1/5 dose ID by jet injector
Schedule / timing: Single booster with assessment at 28 days and 1 year
Primary endpoint: Difference in post-vaccination log2 GMC compared with reference group
Immunogenicity endpoint: Neutralizing antibody GMC at 28 days and 1 year
Safety endpoint: Pain, erythema, swelling, soreness after vaccination
Non-inferiority margin: Difference of 1 serum dilution in microneutralization assay
NI result: ID fractional dose not statistically different from full dose but stringent NI criterion not met; IM fractional dose inferior
Key numerical result(s): Jet injection was less painful (87% no pain vs 60% with needle) but caused more erythema and swelling. At 28 days, ID-JI-0.1 GMC were slightly lower than reference and did not meet the pre-specified NI criterion; after 1 year, differences were no longer apparent. IM fractional-dose needle vaccination was inferior.
Reason not meta-analyzable: Adult booster study using GMC differences and a device comparator; may not align with infant primary-series outcomes.
Data extraction notes: Good comparator paper for whether intradermal delivery rescues dose reduction better than intramuscular fractional dosing. Sources: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23770332/ ; https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264410X13007640
Screening note: Primary intradermal fractional IPV booster study
TL;DR / study gist: In healthy adults, a one-fifth IPV booster given intradermally with a jet injector came close to full-dose intramuscular boosting, while a one-fifth intramuscular dose clearly underperformed. The intradermal jet injector was less painful but caused more transient local skin reactions. This paper supports intradermal rather than intramuscular use when IPV doses are fractionated.
Original abstract: For global eradication of poliomyelitis, inactivated poliovirus vaccine (IPV) needs to become available in all countries. Using fractional-doses (reduced-doses) may impact affordability and optimize the utilization of the production capacity. Intradermal administration has the potential to lower the dose without reducing immunogenicity. A needle-free jet injector may be a reliable way to administer vaccines intradermally. The primary objective of this randomized controlled trial was to compare the immunogenicity and tolerability of fractional-dose intradermal IPV (Netherlands Vaccine Institute, NVI) booster vaccination administered with a jet injector (PharmaJet) to full-dose and fractional-dose intramuscular vaccination with a needle and syringe. Immunogenicity was assessed by comparing the differences in the post-vaccination log2 geometric mean concentrations of neutralizing antibodies (GMC) between the study groups. A total of 125 Dutch adult volunteers with a well-documented vaccination history were randomized to one of four groups: full-dose intramuscular needle (IM-NS-0.5), full-dose intramuscular jet injector (IM-JI-0.5), 1/5th dose intramuscular needle (IM-NS-0.1), 1/5th dose intradermal jet injector (ID-JI-0.1). Vaccination with the JI was less painful (87% no pain) than vaccination with a NS (60% no pain), but caused more transient erythema (JI 85%, NS 24%) and swelling (JI 50%, NS 5%). Intradermal vaccination caused less vaccination site soreness (ID 16%, IM 52%). At baseline all subjects had seroprotective antibody concentrations. After 28 days, GMC were slightly lower in the ID-JI-0.1 group than in the reference group (IM-NS-0.5). The differences were not statistically significant, but the stringent non-inferiority criterion (i.e. a difference of 1 serum dilution in the microneutralization assay) was not met. After one year, differences in GMC were no longer apparent. In contrast, intramuscular vaccination with a fractional dose administered with a needle (IM-NS-0.1) was statistically inferior to full-dose intramuscular vaccination. This shows that intradermal but not intramuscular delivery of fractional-dose IPV may be sufficient for routine polio vaccination.

Title: Priming after a fractional dose of inactivated poliovirus vaccine
Authors: Resik, Sonia; Tejeda, Alina; Sutter, Roland W.; Diaz, Manuel; Sarmiento, Luis; Alemañi, Nilda; Garcia, Gloria; Fonseca, Magilé; Hung, Lai Heng; Kahn, Anna-Lea; Burton, Anthony; Landaverde, J. Mauricio; Aylward, R. Bruce
Journal / source: The New England Journal of Medicine
Vaccine / intervention: Inactivated poliovirus vaccine, fractional intradermal vs full intramuscular
Special population: Cuban infants in primary immunization schedule
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): 1/5 dose intradermal IPV
Schedule / timing: Two-dose schedule at 4 and 8 months with interim blood draw 7 days after dose 2 and final blood draw 30 days after dose 2
Primary endpoint: Single-dose seroconversion, priming, and cumulative two-dose seroconversion
Immunogenicity endpoint: Neutralizing antibody seroconversion and priming for poliovirus types 1-3
Safety endpoint: Adverse events after vaccination
NI result: Lower single-dose and two-dose seroconversion than full-dose IM, but >90% priming and high cumulative response after two doses
Key numerical result(s): After one fractional dose, seroconversion was 16.6%, 47.1%, and 14.7% for types 1-3 versus 46.6%, 62.8%, and 32.0% with one full dose. Priming after one dose exceeded 89% for all serotypes, and cumulative two-dose seroconversion after fractional IPV was 93.6%, 98.1%, and 93.0%.
Data extraction notes: Important core paper because it distinguishes priming from immediate seroconversion and supports two-dose fIPV strategies despite weaker single-dose response. Sources: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23363495/ ; https://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/NEJMoa1202541
Screening note: Primary fractional IPV priming study
TL;DR / study gist: This infant IPV trial shows why fractional dosing remained attractive despite weaker early seroconversion. A single one-fifth intradermal dose underperformed a full intramuscular dose, but it still primed immune responses in over 90% of infants and produced high cumulative seroconversion after two doses. It is a core paper for understanding the logic behind two-dose fIPV schedules.
Original abstract: BACKGROUND: To reduce the costs of maintaining a poliovirus immunization base in low-income areas, we assessed the extent of priming immune responses after the administration of inactivated poliovirus vaccine (IPV). METHODS: We compared the immunogenicity and reactogenicity of a fractional dose of IPV (one fifth of a full dose) administered intradermally with a full dose administered intramuscularly in Cuban infants at the ages of 4 and 8 months. Blood was collected from infants at the ages of 4 months, 8 months, 8 months 7 days, and 8 months 30 days to assess single-dose seroconversion, single-dose priming of immune responses, and two-dose seroconversion. Specimens were tested with a neutralization assay. RESULTS: A total of 320 infants underwent randomization, and 310 infants (96.9%) fulfilled the study requirements. In the group receiving the first fractional dose of IPV, seroconversion to poliovirus types 1, 2, and 3 occurred in 16.6%, 47.1%, and 14.7% of participants, respectively, as compared with 46.6%, 62.8%, and 32.0% in the group receiving the first full dose of IPV (P<0.008 for all comparisons). A priming immune response to poliovirus types 1, 2, and 3 occurred in 90.8%, 94.0%, and 89.6% of participants, respectively, in the group receiving the fractional dose as compared with 97.6%, 98.3%, and 98.1% in the group receiving the full dose (P=0.01 for the comparison with type 3). After the administration of the second dose of IPV in the group receiving fractional doses, cumulative two-dose seroconversion to poliovirus types 1, 2, and 3 occurred in 93.6%, 98.1%, and 93.0% of participants, respectively, as compared with 100.0%, 100.0%, and 99.4% in the group receiving the full dose (P<0.006 for the comparisons of types 1 and 3). The group receiving intradermal injections had the greatest number of adverse events, most of which were minor in intensity and none of which had serious consequences. CONCLUSIONS: This evaluation shows that vaccinating infants with a single fractional dose of IPV can induce priming and seroconversion in more than 90% of immunized infants. (Funded by the World Health Organization and the Pan American Health Organization; Australian New Zealand Clinical Trials Registry number, ACTRN12610001046099.).

Title: Safety and immunogenicity of revaccination with reduced dose intradermal and standard dose intramuscular influenza vaccines in adults 18-64 years of age
Authors: Gorse, Geoffrey J.; Falsey, Ann R.; Johnson, Carol M.; Morrison, Dennis; Fried, David L.; Ervin, John E.; Greenberg, David P.; Ozol-Godfrey, Ayca; Landolfi, Victoria; Tsang, Peter H.
Vaccine / intervention: Seasonal trivalent inactivated influenza vaccine, reduced-dose intradermal vs standard intramuscular revaccination
Special population: Adults aged 18-64 years undergoing annual revaccination
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): Reduced-dose intradermal vaccine (licensed ID formulation)
Schedule / timing: Annual revaccination in consecutive years; outcomes through 7 days for reactogenicity and post-vaccination immunogenicity
Primary endpoint: Comparative immunogenicity and safety of ID vs IM revaccination
Immunogenicity endpoint: Post-vaccination antibody responses
Safety endpoint: Solicited local and systemic reactions within 7 days
NI result: As immunogenic as IM vaccine; effectively non-inferior in abstract-level reporting
Key numerical result(s): ID revaccination caused more erythema, induration, swelling, pruritus, and ecchymosis than IM vaccination, but pain and systemic reactions were similar. The abstract concludes ID vaccine was as immunogenic as IM vaccine for annual revaccination.
Reason not meta-analyzable: Abstract/web source lacks exact denominators and strain-specific antibody outcomes.
Data extraction notes: Abstract/web only. Useful adult revaccination influenza comparison; would need full text for exact sample size and antibody endpoints.
Screening note: Primary reduced-dose intradermal influenza revaccination trial
TL;DR / study gist: In adults aged 18–64 receiving influenza vaccine in consecutive years, the reduced-dose intradermal vaccine produced antibody responses comparable to standard intramuscular revaccination. Local skin reactions were more common with intradermal dosing, but systemic reactions were similar and no major safety concerns emerged.
Original abstract: Results: Reactions were well tolerated and resolved in the first 7 days, but erythema, induration, swelling, pruritus and ecchymosis were reported by more subjects receiving the ID vaccine than the IM vaccine. Compared with receipt of IM vaccine in the previous year, ID vaccine in the previous year led to statistically higher rates of erythema, swelling and induration after IM vaccine in the second year. Injection-site pain and systemic reactions did not differ between ID and IM vaccines. Conclusions: The ID vaccine was as immunogenic as the IM vaccine, and raised no safety concerns. It can be used interchangeably with the IM vaccine for annual revaccination in adults 18-64 years of age in consecutive years without safety concerns.

Title: A reduced-dose seasonal trivalent influenza vaccine is safe and immunogenic in adult and elderly patients in a randomized controlled trial
Authors: Vajo, Zoltan; Tamas, Ferenc; Jankovics, Istvan
Vaccine / intervention: Whole-virion inactivated adjuvanted seasonal trivalent influenza vaccine, reduced dose vs conventional dose
Comparator (full dose / standard / other: Conventional 15 μg trivalent influenza vaccine dose
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): 6 μg trivalent influenza vaccine dose
Schedule / timing: Single seasonal vaccination with follow-up for safety and post-vaccination HAI testing
Primary endpoint: Safety and immunogenicity compared with licensing criteria
Immunogenicity endpoint: Hemagglutination inhibition antibody response
Safety endpoint: Adverse events after vaccination
NI result: Not explicitly framed as NI; reduced-dose vaccine met licensing immunogenicity criteria
Key numerical result(s): 234 participants were enrolled: 120 adults and 114 elderly subjects. Participants received either 6 μg or 15 μg seasonal trivalent influenza vaccine, and the reduced-dose vaccine was reported as safe and immunogenic and compliant with licensing criteria.
Reason not meta-analyzable: Reduced-dose IM formulation study with limited abstract-level quantitative data.
Data extraction notes: Useful if broader review includes reduced-dose IM influenza formulations, not only intradermal dose sparing.
Screening note: Primary reduced-dose influenza trial
TL;DR / study gist: This randomized trial tested whether a lower-dose adjuvanted seasonal influenza vaccine could preserve performance in adults and older adults. The 6 μg dose was reported as safe and immunogenic, meeting licensing-style criteria, suggesting some intramuscular antigen reduction may be feasible.
Original abstract: With the recent pandemic of influenza A (H1N1) and vaccine shortages, there has been considerable interest in developing influenza vaccines with reduced doses, allowing for increased production capacity. Here we report a prospective, randomized, double-blind, single-center clinical trial of a reduced-dose whole-virion inactivated, adjuvanted influenza vaccine in adult and elderly volunteers. A total of 234 subjects, including 120 adults (18 to 60 years of age) and 114 elderly subjects (>60 years of age) were enrolled to receive either 6 μg or the conventional 15-μg dose of seasonal trivalent influenza vaccines. The subjects were followed for safety analysis, and serum samples were obtained to assess immunogenicity by hemagglutination inhibition testing. We found that the present reduced-dose vaccine is safe and immunogenic in healthy adult and elderly subjects and triggers immune responses that comply with licensing criteria.

Title: Dose sparing intradermal trivalent influenza (2010/2011) vaccination overcomes reduced immunogenicity of the 2009 H1N1 strain
Authors: Hung, Ivan F. N.; Levin, Yotam; To, Kelvin K. W.; Chan, Kwok-Hung; Zhang, Anna Jinxia; Li, Patrick; Li, Clara; Xu, Ting; Wong, Tin-Yan; Yuen, Kwok-Yung
Vaccine / intervention: Seasonal trivalent inactivated influenza vaccine delivered intradermally at reduced doses
Comparator (full dose / standard / other: Full-dose intramuscular TIV (15 μg/strain) and mid-dose intradermal Intanza 9 μg
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): 3 μg and 9 μg intradermal TIV via MicronJet600
Schedule / timing: Single vaccination with baseline and day-21 serology
Primary endpoint: Non-inferiority of low-dose ID vaccination versus full-dose IM and commercial ID comparator
Immunogenicity endpoint: HAI and microneutralization antibody titers at day 21
Safety endpoint: Solicited adverse reactions
Non-inferiority margin: Not clearly stated in available abstract text
NI result: Low-dose ID vaccination reportedly overcame poor H1N1 immunogenicity; exact NI results require full text
Key numerical result(s): Participants were randomized to ID3, ID9, INT9, or IM15 groups. The study measured HAI and microneutralization titers at day 21 and concluded dose-sparing ID vaccination improved responses to the 2009 H1N1 strain in elderly/chronically ill adults.
Reason not meta-analyzable: Need full text for exact group sizes and strain-specific endpoint data.
Data extraction notes: Promising dose-sparing influenza study but needs full-text verification of NI framework and results.
Screening note: Primary dose-sparing intradermal influenza study
TL;DR / study gist: Among elderly and chronically ill adults, reduced-dose intradermal trivalent influenza vaccination appeared able to preserve or improve immunogenicity compared with conventional approaches, particularly for the 2009 H1N1 component. The study is highly relevant for dose sparing, but the exact non-inferiority details and group-level numbers need full-text checking.
Original abstract: We hypothesized that low dose intradermal vaccination of the trivalent influenza vaccine (TIV) delivered by the MicronJet600™ (NanoPass Technologies, Israel) would be non-inferior to the full dose intramuscular and mid dose Intanza® vaccination in the elderly and the chronically ill adults. We performed a prospective randomized trial on elderly and chronically ill adults. Subjects were randomly assigned into 4 groups. Groups ID3 and ID9 received reduced dose ID TIV (3 μg and 9 μg of hemagglutinin per strain respectively) delivered by MicronJet600™. Group INT9 received reduced dose ID TIV (9 μg) delivered by Soluvia™. Control group IM15 received a full dose IM TIV (15 μg). We measured antibody titers by hemagglutination inhibition and microneutralization assays at baseline and day 21.

Title: Phase 4 randomized trial of intradermal low-antigen-content inactivated influenza vaccine versus standard-dose intramuscular vaccine in HIV-1-infected adults
Authors: Ansaldi, Filippo; Valle, Laura; de Florentiis, Daniela; Parodi, Valentina; Murdaca, Giuseppe; Bruzzone, Bianca; Durando, Paolo; Setti, Maurizio; Icardi, Giancarlo
Vaccine / intervention: Intanza 9 μg intradermal influenza vaccine vs standard-dose intramuscular influenza vaccine
Comparator (full dose / standard / other: Standard-dose intramuscular trivalent inactivated influenza vaccine
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): Low-antigen-content intradermal influenza vaccine (9 μg/strain)
Schedule / timing: Seasonal vaccination with follow-up for persistence of antibody responses
Sample size randomized: Not clearly stated in abstract snippet
Sample size analyzed: Not clearly stated in abstract snippet
Primary endpoint: Safety, tolerability, immunogenicity, and persistence of antibody response
Immunogenicity endpoint: Post-vaccination antibody responses and persistence
Safety endpoint: Safety and tolerability
Non-inferiority margin: Not clearly stated in available web text
NI result: Appears comparable, but exact NI conclusion requires full text
Key numerical result(s): The study evaluated 9 μg intradermal Intanza in HIV-1-infected adults against standard IM influenza vaccine and focused on immunogenicity, tolerability, and antibody persistence.
Reason not meta-analyzable: Need full text for group size, exact immunogenicity results, and formal NI framework.
Data extraction notes: Relevant special-population influenza trial. Good candidate for full-text follow-up if HIV subgroup evidence matters.
Screening note: Primary low-antigen-content intradermal influenza trial
TL;DR / study gist: This phase 4 trial asked whether a lower-antigen intradermal influenza vaccine could work as well as standard intramuscular vaccination in adults with HIV-1. It looks relevant and potentially supportive of dose sparing in immunocompromised adults, but the abstract-level sources do not provide enough numeric detail for full extraction.
Original abstract: The aim of the study was to explore the safety, tolerability and immunogenicity profiles and the persistence of antibody responses of Intanza® 9 μg, in HIV-1-infected adults receiving antiretroviral therapy. This study evaluated intradermal trivalent inactivated split influenza vaccine, with a lower antigen content (9 μg/strain), compared with standard-dose intramuscular influenza vaccine in HIV-1-infected adults.

Title: Randomized controlled study of fractional doses of inactivated poliovirus vaccine administered intradermally with a needle in the Philippines
Authors: Cadorna-Carlos, Josefina; Vidor, Emmanuel; Bonnet, Marie-Claude
Vaccine / intervention: Inactivated poliovirus vaccine (fractional intradermal vs full intramuscular)
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): 1/5 dose IPV given intradermally by needle
Schedule / timing: Primary series at 6, 10, and 14 weeks; booster at 15–18 months
Primary endpoint: Non-inferiority of fractional ID IPV vs full IM IPV
Immunogenicity endpoint: Post-primary and post-booster anti-polio titers to types 1, 2, and 3
Safety endpoint: Adverse events including injection-site reactions
NI result: ID route was non-inferior to IM route after primary series
Key numerical result(s): After the primary series, ≥99–100% of participants had anti-polio titers ≥8 for types 1–3. Antibody persistence was 83–100% depending on group, booster responses were strongly anamnestic in all groups, and injection-site erythema was higher in the ID group.
Data extraction notes: Strong abstract-level poliovirus study with explicit non-inferiority framing.
Screening note: Primary human study of fractional/reduced-dose vaccine regimen
TL;DR / study gist: In Filipino infants, one-fifth-dose IPV given intradermally by needle performed very well against full-dose intramuscular IPV. Seroprotection after the primary series was essentially universal, booster responses were strong, and the main trade-off was more local erythema with intradermal injection.
Original abstract: Objective: Comparison of a fractional inactivated poliovirus vaccine (IPV) dose administered intradermally (ID) to a full dose administered intramuscularly (IM). Methods: Healthy Filipino infants were randomized to receive IPV as either a fractional (1/5th) dose ID by needle injection or a full dose IM at 6, 10, and 14 weeks and a booster at 15-18 months of age. Pre- and post-vaccination anti-polio 1, 2, and 3 titers were estimated. Adverse events were monitored throughout the study. Results: Following primary series vaccination, anti-polio 1, 2, and 3 titers were ≥8 (1/dil) in 99-100% of participants, and the ID route was non-inferior to the IM route. Depending on the study group, antibody persistence was detected in 83-100% of participants, and the booster dose resulted in a strong anamnestic response in all groups. The incidence of adverse events in each group was similar, except for injection-site erythema (higher in the ID group). Conclusions: Primary series and booster vaccination of a fractional IPV dose administered by the ID route was highly immunogenic and well tolerated. These data confirm the medical validity of using fractional ID doses of IPV.

Title: Immunogenicity and safety of intradermal influenza vaccine in children
Authors: Esposito, Susanna; Daleno, Cristina; Picciolli, Irene; Tagliaferri, Laura; Scala, Alessia; Prunotto, Giulia; Montinaro, Valentina; Galeone, Carlotta; Principi, Nicola
Vaccine / intervention: Trivalent influenza vaccine, intradermal vs intramuscular/virosomal comparator
Special population: Previously primed healthy children aged ≥3 years
Comparator (full dose / standard / other: Full IM dose (15 μg/strain) of virosomal-adjuvanted influenza vaccine
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): 9 μg and 15 μg ID-TIV per strain
Schedule / timing: Single vaccination with post-vaccination serology
Primary endpoint: Compare immunogenicity and safety of different ID doses vs full IM comparator
Immunogenicity endpoint: Seroconversion, seroprotection, and GMT changes to A/H1N1, A/H3N2, and B
Safety endpoint: Local and systemic reactions
Non-inferiority margin: Not explicitly stated; comparative superiority/non-inferiority implied
NI result: ID doses were similar for A strains and better for B; 15 μg ID appeared strongest overall
Key numerical result(s): 112 previously primed children were randomized. A/H1N1 and A/H3N2 seroconversion and seroprotection were ≥90% with no significant between-group differences; responses to influenza B were significantly higher in the ID groups, and GMT against B was highest with 15 μg ID.
Reason not meta-analyzable: Comparator is virosomal-adjuvanted IM vaccine; exact arm-level denominators may be needed from full text.
Data extraction notes: Relevant pediatric influenza route/dose study; not a clean same-product comparison.
Screening note: Randomized pediatric influenza study comparing lower-dose intradermal with standard-dose intramuscular vaccination.
TL;DR / study gist: In previously primed children aged 3 years or older, intradermal influenza vaccination worked at least as well as the intramuscular comparator for the A strains and appeared better for the B strain. Local reactions were more common with intradermal dosing, but systemic reactions were uncommon.
Original abstract: In order to compare the immunogenicity and safety of different doses of trivalent influenza vaccine (TIV) administered intradermallly (ID) with those evoked by a full dose of intramuscular (IM) virosomal-adjuvanted influenza vaccine (VA-TIV), 112 previously primed healthy children aged ≥ 3 years were randomised to receive 9 μg or 15 μg of each strain of ID-TIV, or a full IM dose (15 μg of each strain) of VA-TIV. The A/H1N1 and A/H3N2 seroconversion and seroprotection rates were ≥ 90% and geometric mean titres increased 3.2-14.9 times without statistically significant between-group differences; however, seroconversion and seroprotection rates against the B strain were significantly higher in the children receiving either ID-TIV dose without differences between them. GMT against B virus was significantly higher in the children receiving the highest dose. Local reactions were significantly more common among the children receiving either ID-TIV dose, but systemic reactions were relatively uncommon in all groups.

Title: Randomized controlled trial of dose response to influenza vaccine in children aged 6 to 23 months
Authors: Skowronski, Danuta M.; Hottes, Travis S.; Chong, Mei; De Serres, Gaston; Scheifele, David W.; Ward, Brian J.; Halperin, Scott A.; Janjua, Naveed Z.; Chan, Tracy; Sabaiduc, Suzana; Petric, Martin
Vaccine / intervention: Split trivalent inactivated influenza vaccine, full vs half dose
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): Comparison framed as full dose 0.5 mL vs half dose 0.25 mL; reduced arm is half dose
Schedule / timing: Two doses of 2008–2009 split TIV with serology 27–45 days after second injection
Primary endpoint: Superiority of full versus half dose based on >10% increase in seroprotection rates
Immunogenicity endpoint: Post-vaccination seroprotection (HAI ≥40) to each vaccine component
Safety endpoint: Fever ≥38°C within 3 days after either injection
NI result: Not applicable; full dose superior in infants for H3N2 and B/Yamagata, but not toddlers
Key numerical result(s): In toddlers, seroprotection exceeded 85% for all components with no significant dose difference. In infants, full dose beat half dose for H3N2 (75.4% vs 47.6%) and B/Yamagata (70.2% vs 41.3%), but not H1N1 (71.9% vs 54.0%). Fever was not increased with full-dose vaccination (5.6% vs 12.7% combined).
Data extraction notes: Useful pediatric reduced-dose IM influenza trial, although not intradermal.
Screening note: Primary randomized trial directly comparing half-dose versus full-dose influenza vaccine in young children.
TL;DR / study gist: In children aged 6–23 months, half-dose influenza vaccine was enough for toddlers but looked weaker in infants. Full-dose vaccination improved H3N2 and B responses in infants without increasing fever, suggesting age matters when considering dose reduction.
Original abstract: Objectives: We assessed whether 2 full versus 2 half-doses of trivalent inactivated influenza vaccine (TIV) could improve immunogenicity without increasing reactogenicity in infants (aged 6-11 months) and toddlers (aged 12-23 months). Methods: Previously unimmunized infants and toddlers were separately randomly assigned to receive 2 full (0.5-mL) or 2 half (0.25-mL) doses of 2008-2009 split TIV. Sera were collected at enrollment and at 27 to 45 days after the second injection. Parents recorded adverse events after each injection. The primary immunogenicity outcome was superiority of the full versus the half-dose based on a >10% increase in rates of seroprotection. Results: In per-protocol analyses, 252 participants (full dose: n = 124; half-dose: n = 128) were included. In toddlers, postimmunization seroprotection rates exceeded 85% for all 3 vaccine components without significant difference by dose. In infants, the full dose induced higher responses for all 3 vaccine components, meeting the 10% test of superiority for the H3N2 and B/Yamagata components but not H1N1. Rates of fever were not increased among full- versus half-dose recipients in either age group.

Title: Fractional Doses of Inactivated Poliovirus Vaccine in Oman
Authors: Mohammed, Ali Jafer; AlAwaidy, Salah; Bawikar, Shyam; Kurup, Padmamohan J.; Elamir, Emadaldin; Shaban, Mahmoud M. A.; Sharif, Sharif M.; Avoort, Harrie G. A. M. van der; Pallansch, Mark A.; Malankar, Pradeep; Burton, Anthony; Sreevatsava, Meghana; Sutter, Roland W.
Vaccine / intervention: Inactivated poliovirus vaccine, fractional intradermal vs full intramuscular
Outcome domains: Immunogenicity; safety/reactogenicity; viral shedding after challenge
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): 0.1 mL (1/5 dose) IPV intradermally via needle-free jet injector
Schedule / timing: 2, 4, and 6 months with monovalent type 1 OPV challenge at 7 months
Primary endpoint: Immunogenicity and reactogenicity of fractional vs full-dose IPV
Immunogenicity endpoint: Seroconversion and antibody titers to poliovirus types 1–3 after 3-dose schedule
Clinical endpoint: Type 1 poliovirus excretion after challenge OPV dose
Safety endpoint: Hospitalizations and reactogenicity
NI result: Fractional dose achieved high seroconversion but lower type 2 seroconversion and lower median titers than full dose
Key numerical result(s): Seroconversion after 3 doses in the fractional group was 97.3%, 95.7%, and 97.9% for types 1–3 versus 100% for all serotypes in the full-dose group; only the type 2 difference was significant. Median titers were lower in the fractional group, and 74.8% vs 63.1% shed type 1 virus after challenge.
Data extraction notes: Landmark infant IPV trial; strong for extraction and likely for quantitative synthesis.
Screening note: Primary human influenza study comparing reduced-dose intradermal vs standard intramuscular vaccination.
TL;DR / study gist: In Omani infants, one-fifth-dose intradermal IPV generated very high seroconversion but still underperformed full-dose intramuscular IPV on antibody titers and type 2 seroconversion. It is a key proof-of-concept study showing that dose sparing can work, but not always without some immunogenicity trade-offs.
Original abstract: Background: We conducted a clinical trial of fractional doses of inactivated poliovirus vaccine administered to infants in Oman, in order to evaluate strategies for making the vaccine affordable for use in developing countries. Methods: We compared fractional doses of inactivated poliovirus vaccine (0.1 ml, representing one fifth of a full dose) given intradermally with the use of a needle-free jet injector device, with full doses given intramuscularly. Infants were randomly assigned at birth to receive either a fractional dose or a full dose of IPV at 2, 4, and 6 months. Results: A total of 400 infants were randomized, of whom 373 fulfilled the study requirements. Thirty days after the three-dose schedule, seroconversion to types 1, 2, and 3 poliovirus was 97.3%, 95.7%, and 97.9% in the fractional-dose group versus 100% for all serotypes in the full-dose group; median titers were significantly lower in the fractional-dose group. At 7 months, 74.8% of infants in the fractional-dose group and 63.1% in the full-dose group excreted type 1 poliovirus after challenge. There were no significant safety differences between groups.

Title: Immunogenicity and safety of intradermal influenza vaccination in healthy older adults
Authors: Chi, Ru-Chien; Rock, Michael T.; Neuzil, Kathleen M.
Journal / source: Clinical Infectious Diseases: An Official Publication of the Infectious Diseases Society of America
Vaccine / intervention: Trivalent inactivated influenza vaccine, reduced-dose ID or IM vs full-dose IM
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): Reduced-dose 9 μg IM, reduced-dose 9 μg ID, and two 4.5 μg ID injections
Schedule / timing: Single vaccination, with revaccination of reduced-dose groups using full-dose IM later
Primary endpoint: Compare immunogenicity of reduced-dose ID vs full-dose IM vaccination
Immunogenicity endpoint: Post-vaccination HAI seroprotection rates
Safety endpoint: Local reactions such as redness, swelling, itching
NI result: 60% dose by IM or ID elicited generally similar antibody responses to full-dose IM
Key numerical result(s): Among 257 adults aged 65 years, seroprotection rates for H1N1 were 65.6%, 57.8%, 68.9%, and 67.2% across standard IM, reduced IM, reduced ID, and two reduced ID injections; for H3N2 they were 76.6%, 75.0%, 75.4%, and 75.0%; for influenza B they were 26.6%, 17.2%, 16.4%, and 25.0%. Local reactions were more frequent with ID vaccination.
Data extraction notes: Useful older-adult influenza dosing study with several reduced-dose arms.
Screening note: Randomized study including a 60% intradermal influenza dose versus standard intramuscular dose.
TL;DR / study gist: In healthy adults aged 65 years, giving only 60% of the influenza vaccine dose by either intramuscular or intradermal route produced broadly similar antibody responses to full-dose intramuscular vaccination. The main downside was more local skin reactions with intradermal injection.
Original abstract: Background: Influenza vaccine immunogenicity is suboptimal in older persons. Intradermal vaccination may be a promising alternative to intramuscular vaccination. Methods: This randomized trial compared the immunogenicity of 60% dose intradermal influenza vaccination to standard intramuscular vaccination of full-dose or 60% dose vaccine. Participants who received reduced-dose vaccine were revaccinated with full-dose IM vaccine. Results: 257 healthy adults aged 65 years received standard-dose IM vaccine, reduced-dose IM vaccine, reduced-dose ID vaccine, or two reduced-dose ID injections. Respective seroprotection rates were 65.6%, 57.8%, 68.9%, and 67.2% against A/H1N1; 76.6%, 75.0%, 75.4%, and 75.0% against A/H3N2; and 26.6%, 17.2%, 16.4%, and 25.0% against influenza B. Subsequent full-dose IM revaccination of reduced-dose recipients did not improve seroprotection rates. Local reactions were significantly more frequent among recipients of ID injections. Conclusion: Influenza vaccine at 60% dose by either IM or ID route elicited antibody responses generally similar to full-dose IM vaccination among healthy elderly persons.

Title: Pneumococcal nasopharyngeal carriage following reduced doses of a 7-valent pneumococcal conjugate vaccine and a 23-valent pneumococcal polysaccharide vaccine booster
Authors: Russell, F. M.; Carapetis, J. R.; Satzke, C.; Tikoduadua, L.; Waqatakirewa, L.; Chandra, R.; Seduadua, A.; Oftadeh, S.; Cheung, Y. B.; Gilbert, G. L.; Mulholland, E. K.
Vaccine / intervention: 7-valent pneumococcal conjugate vaccine with reduced-dose primary series ± 23vPPS booster
Comparator (full dose / standard / other: 0, 1, 2, or 3 infant PCV doses with/without 23vPPS booster
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): Reduced number of infant PCV doses (1 or 2 doses vs 3-dose schedule)
Schedule / timing: Infant randomization at 6 weeks; NP swabs at 6, 9, 12, and 17 months; 23vPPS at 12 months in half of each group
Primary endpoint: Effect of reduced-dose PCV schedule on nasopharyngeal carriage
Immunogenicity endpoint: Not primary in this paper
Safety endpoint: Not a main endpoint in abstract
NI result: Not applicable
Key numerical result(s): Fijian infants were randomized to 0, 1, 2, or 3 PCV doses, with half receiving 23vPPS at 12 months. At 17 months, within-dose-group comparisons by 23vPPS booster were not significant (all P>0.45), and the study focused on vaccine-type carriage outcomes.
Reason not meta-analyzable: Reduced-dose schedule/carriage study rather than a direct fractional-antigen comparison; detailed carriage data would need full text.
Data extraction notes: Relevant if the review includes reduced-dose schedules as dose-sparing; less relevant if limited to fractional antigen dose.
Screening note: Pilot human study explicitly testing full-dose versus reduced (20%) intradermal/intramuscular HPV vaccination.
TL;DR / study gist: This Fiji trial asked whether giving fewer infant PCV doses, with or without a later polysaccharide booster, changed pneumococcal carriage. It is useful for a broad dose-sparing review, but it is more about reduced schedules than fractional antigen dosing.
Original abstract: This study evaluated the effect of a reduced-dose 7-valent pneumococcal conjugate vaccine primary series followed by a 23-valent pneumococcal polysaccharide booster on nasopharyngeal pneumococcal carriage. Fijian infants aged 6 weeks were randomized to receive 0, 1, 2, or 3 PCV doses; within each group, half received 23vPPS at 12 months. Nasopharyngeal swabs were taken at 6, 9, 12, and 17 months and cultured for Streptococcus pneumoniae. At 17 months, there were no significant carriage differences between those receiving or not receiving 23vPPS within the same PCV dosage group.

Title: Randomized Controlled Clinical Trial of Fractional Doses of Inactivated Poliovirus Vaccine Administered Intradermally by Needle-Free Device in Cuba
Authors: Resik, Sonia; Tejeda, Alina; Mas Lago, Pedro; Diaz, Manuel; Carmenates, Ania; Sarmiento, Luis; Alemañi, Nilda; Galindo, Belkis; Burton, Anthony; Friede, Martin; Landaverde, Mauricio; Sutter, Roland W.
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): Fractional-dose IPV 0.1 mL (1/5 full dose) intradermal by needle-free jet injector
Schedule / timing: At birth randomization; IPV at 6, 10, and 14 weeks; immunogenicity 30 days after dose 3
Primary endpoint: Seroconversion after 3-dose IPV schedule and reactogenicity
Immunogenicity endpoint: Seroconversion and median titers for poliovirus types 1, 2, and 3
Safety endpoint: Reactogenicity/adverse events
NI result: Did not demonstrate comparable immunogenicity; fractional ID arm was inferior to full-dose IM
Key numerical result(s): 30 days after dose 3, seroconversion in fractional vs full-dose arms was 52.9% vs 89.3% for type 1, 85.0% vs 95.5% for type 2, and 69.0% vs 98.9% for type 3; median titers were significantly lower in the ID arm.
Reason not meta-analyzable: Abstract/web sources provide headline seroconversion results but not all arm-level details or exact variance estimates.
Data extraction notes: Abstract/web only. Important early infant fIPV jet-injector trial; schedule at 6/10/14 weeks appears suboptimal for immunogenicity.
Screening note: Primary human study of fractional/reduced-dose vaccine regimen
TL;DR / study gist: In Cuban infants, three 1/5-dose intradermal IPV doses delivered by jet injector were feasible but clearly less immunogenic than standard full-dose intramuscular IPV. The study supports the practicality of fractional IPV delivery, but not equivalence of this particular schedule.
Original abstract: Background. As part of an evaluation of strategies to make inactivated poliovirus vaccine (IPV) affordable for developing countries, we conducted a clinical trial of fractional doses of IPV in Cuba.Methods. We compared the immunogenicity and reactogenicity of fractional-dose IPV (0.1 mL, or 1/5 of a full dose) given intradermally using a needle-free jet injector device compared with full doses given intramuscularly. Subjects were randomized at birth to receive IPV at 6, 10, and 14 weeks.Results. A total of 471 subjects were randomized to the 2 study groups, and 364 subjects fulfilled the study requirements. No significant differences at baseline were detected. Thirty days after completing the 3-dose schedule of IPV, 52.9%, 85.0%, and 69.0% of subjects in the fractional-dose IPV arm seroconverted for poliovirus types 1, 2, and 3, respectively, whereas 89.3%, 95.5%, and 98.9% of subjects in the full-dose IPV arm seroconverted for poliovirus types 1, 2, and 3, respectively (all comparisons, P<.001). the median titers of each poliovirus serotype were significantly lower in the intradermal arm than in the intramuscular arm (P<.001). only minor local adverse effects and no moderate or serious adverse events were reported.Conclusions. This large-scale evaluation demonstrates the feasibility of fractional doses of IPV given intradermally as an antigen-sparing strategy but also shows that IPV given to infants at 6, 10, and 14 weeks of age results in suboptimal immunogenicity (especially for the fractional-dose arm).Trial Registration. Controlled-trials.com identifier: ISRCTN19673867.

Title: Dose sparing strategy with intradermal influenza vaccination in patients with solid cancer
Authors: Jo, Yu Mi; Song, Joon Young; Hwang, In Sook; Lee, Jacob; Oh, Sang Cheul; Kim, Jun Suk; Kim, Sung Ran; Kim, Woo Joo; Cheong, Hee Jin
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): Half-dose intradermal seasonal influenza vaccine
Schedule / timing: Single seasonal vaccination; immunogenicity assessed 4–6 weeks after vaccination
Primary endpoint: Comparative immunogenicity of half-dose ID vs full-dose IM vaccination
Immunogenicity endpoint: Hemagglutination inhibition response, seroconversion factor, seroresponse and seroprotection rates for A/H1N1, A/H3N2, and B
Safety endpoint: Adverse events, especially local skin reactions and serious events
NI result: Comparable by abstract-level reporting; no significant difference between routes
Key numerical result(s): There was no significant difference in HI response or titer increase for A/H1N1, A/H3N2, or B 4–6 weeks post-vaccination. Seroresponse rates were >40% and seroprotection rates >70% for all strains regardless of route; local skin reactions were more frequent after ID vaccination (32.7% vs 9.1%), with no serious adverse events.
Reason not meta-analyzable: Abstract/web sources do not provide sample size or arm-level quantitative results.
Data extraction notes: Abstract/web only. Very relevant special-population influenza dose-sparing study, but full text would be needed for denominators and exact antibody outcomes.
Screening note: Primary human influenza study of reduced-dose intradermal vs standard intramuscular vaccination in COPD patients.
TL;DR / study gist: Among adults with solid cancer in Korea, half-dose intradermal influenza vaccination appeared to produce immune responses similar to full-dose intramuscular vaccination. Safety was acceptable, with more local reactions but no serious vaccine-related events reported.
Original abstract: Influenza vaccine is considered to reduce influenza-related morbidity and mortality in patients with underlying chronic medical conditions. Because of fear of vaccine shortage during an influenza pandemic, several antigen sparing strategies have been investigated. The immunogenicity of intradermal influenza vaccination with one half the antigenic contents was compared to that of conventional intramuscular vaccination in patients with solid cancer, and adverse events were assessed after vaccination. There was no significant difference between the injection routes in the hemagglutinin inhibition (HI) response and increase in the titer of A/H1N1, A/H3N2, and B 4-6 weeks after the vaccination; seroconversion factors increased by more than 2.5-fold. Seroresponse rates were more than 40% and seroprotection rates were above 70% against all three influenza strains irrespective of the vaccination routes. No serious events were observed, and local skin reactions were more frequent in the intradermal injection recipients than in the intramuscular recipients (32.7% vs. 9.1%). This study shows that intradermal injection of one half the dose of a commercial influenza vaccine elicits immune responses comparable to those elicited by a full dose of intramuscular vaccine among cancer patients, and it can be tolerated without serious adverse reactions.

Title: Immunogenicity and safety of low dose virosomal adjuvanted influenza vaccine administered intradermally compared to intramuscular full dose administration
Authors: Künzi, Valérie; Klap, Jaco M.; Seiberling, Michael K.; Herzog, Christian; Hartmann, Katharina; Kürsteiner, Oliver; Kompier, Ronald; Grimaldi, Roberto; Goudsmit, Jaap
Study design: Randomized partially blinded phase II trial
Outcome domains: Immunogenicity; Safety; Clinical efficacy/effectiveness; Follow-up / persistence
Comparator (full dose / standard / other: 15 μg HA/strain intramuscular trivalent influenza vaccine
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): 3.0, 4.5, and 6.0 μg HA/strain intradermal virosomal-adjuvanted trivalent influenza vaccine
Schedule / timing: Single vaccination; HAI measured before and 3 weeks after vaccination; safety follow-up for 4 days post-vaccination
Primary endpoint: Immunogenicity and safety of reduced-dose intradermal virosomal influenza vaccine versus full-dose IM vaccine
Immunogenicity endpoint: HAI seroconversion rates and fulfillment of EMEA relicensure criteria
Safety endpoint: Investigator and self-reported adverse events
NI result: Broadly comparable immunogenicity across reduced-dose ID and full-dose IM groups
Key numerical result(s): 224 healthy adults were randomized. Three weeks after vaccination, all groups met EMEA relicensure requirements; seroconversion rates ranged from 50.9% to 85.5% after ID vaccination and 70.4% to 87.0% after IM vaccination.
Reason not meta-analyzable: Multi-arm reduced-dose study with limited abstract-level arm-specific results.
Data extraction notes: Abstract/web only. Useful influenza antigen-sparing study with multiple reduced-dose ID arms; full text needed for exact by-strain arm-level values.
Screening note: Primary low-dose intradermal influenza comparison
TL;DR / study gist: This phase II trial tested several low-dose intradermal virosomal influenza vaccine doses against a standard full-dose intramuscular comparator in healthy adults. All groups met relicensure-style immunogenicity criteria, suggesting reduced intradermal doses could be a viable antigen-sparing strategy.
Original abstract: BACKGROUND: Despite the established benefit of intramuscular (i.m.) influenza vaccination, new adjuvants and delivery methods for comparable or improved immunogenicity are being explored. Intradermal (i.d.) antigen administration is hypothesized to initiate an efficient immune response at reduced antigen doses similar to that observed after i.m. full dose vaccination. METHODS: In a randomized, partially blinded phase II study 224, healthy adults aged >or=18 to <or=60 years were randomly assigned to four groups and received trivalent influenza vaccine at single doses of 3.0, 4.5 and 6.0 microg hemagglutinin (HA) antigen of each influenza virus strain via i.d. injection or 15.0 microg HA of each influenza strain via i.m. delivery. Serum anti-influenza virus antibodies were determined by hemagglutination inhibition (HI) assay before and 3 weeks after vaccination. Safety assessments were made at baseline and at the follow-up visit by the investigators and for a 4-day period post-vaccination by the subjects themselves. RESULTS: The EMEA requirements for re-licensing of influenza vaccines were fulfilled in all groups 3 weeks after vaccination, irrespective of dose and mode of administration. High seroconversion rates were observed in all study groups and for all strains ranging from 50.9 to 85.5% and 70.4 to 87.0% after i.d. and i.m. vaccination, respectively. Seroprotection rates for the A strains A/Solomon Islands and A/Wisconsin were generally higher compared to the B/Malaysia strain and ranged from 89.1 to 98.2% across the i.d. groups. Similar rates of 96.3% for the A/Solomon Islands and 94.4% for the A/Wisconsin strain were observed in the i.m. group. Seroprotection rates for the B/Malaysia strain were 65.5, 83.0 and 72.7% after i.d. administration of 3.0, 4.5, and 6.0 microg HA of each strain, respectively, compared to a seroprotection rate of 85.2% in the i.m. group. In addition, marked increases in geometric mean titer (GMT) were observed across the groups for all influenza virus strains ranging from 6.9 to 70.5 for i.d. and from 16.9 to 56.5 for i.m. antigen delivery. Both routes of administration were well tolerated. Systemic reactions were broadly similar across the groups. With respect to local reactions the frequency of injection site pain and ecchymosis were significantly lower following i.d. vaccination, while other local reactions such as erythema occurred at higher rates with i.d. than with i.m. vaccine administration, as expected due to the mechanism of action for the i.d. route. CONCLUSIONS: The virosomal adjuvanted influenza vaccine (Inflexal V) was shown to be overall highly immunogenic and well tolerated when given i.d. at reduced doses to healthy adults, eliciting an immune response similar to that observed with full dose i.m. administration and thus suggesting a promising antigen-sparing strategy for universal influenza vaccination against endemic influenza. TRIAL REGISTRATION: ISRCTN registry number: 33950739.

Title: Intradermal influenza vaccination in immunocompromized patients is immunogenic and feasible
Authors: Gelinck, L. B. S.; van den Bemt, B. J. F.; Marijt, W. a. F.; van der Bijl, A. E.; Visser, L. G.; Cats, H. A.; Rimmelzwaan, G. F.; Kroon, F. P.
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): 0.1 mL intradermal trivalent influenza vaccine
Schedule / timing: Single 2005/2006 seasonal vaccination; HAI before and 28 days after vaccination
Primary endpoint: Comparative GMTs and protection rates in immunocompromised patients and controls
Immunogenicity endpoint: HAI GMTs and protection rates 28 days post-vaccination
Safety endpoint: Adverse events and local skin reactions
NI result: Comparable across groups by abstract-level reporting
Key numerical result(s): 156 immunocompromised patients and 41 healthy controls were randomized. Reduced-dose ID vaccination led to similar GMTs and protection rates within all tested groups compared with standard IM vaccination; local skin reactions after ID vaccination were less frequent and milder in immunocompromised patients than in healthy controls.
Reason not meta-analyzable: Abstract/web sources lack arm-specific counts and detailed strain-level results.
Data extraction notes: Abstract/web only. Broad immunocompromised influenza study spanning anti-TNF, HIV, and HSCT groups; useful for special-population narrative synthesis.
Screening note: Primary human influenza study likely evaluating intradermal dose-sparing in an immunocompromised population.
TL;DR / study gist: In several immunocompromised adult groups, a 0.1 mL intradermal influenza dose produced antibody responses similar to standard intramuscular vaccination. The study suggests fractional intradermal influenza vaccination can be a feasible option even in immunocompromised patients, though healthy controls responded best overall.
Original abstract: BACKGROUND: Many strategies, including intradermal vaccination, have been tested to augment antibody responses upon vaccination. This strategy has not been evaluated in different groups of immunocompromized patients. We conducted a prospective, randomized study to compare the humoral response upon standard intramuscular influenza vaccination with the response upon reduced-dose intradermal vaccination in patients treated with anti-tumor necrosis factor (TNF)-alpha, human immunodeficiency virus (HIV)-infected patients, hematologic stem cell transplantation (HSCT) patients, and healthy controls. METHODS: In total 156 immunocompromized patients and 41 healthy controls were randomized to receive either 0.5mL of the 2005/2006 trivalent influenza vaccine intramuscular or 0.1mL intradermal. Humoral responses, determined by hemagglutination inhibition assay, were measured before and 28 days postvaccination. Geometric mean titers (GMTs) and protection rates (PRs) are reported as primary outcomes, adverse events as a secondary outcome. RESULTS: Reduced-dose intradermal vaccination leads to similar GMTs and PRs, within all tested groups, compared to the standard intramuscular vaccination. Healthy controls yielded significantly better GMTs and PRs than immunocompromized patients. Local skin reactions after intradermal vaccination occurred less frequent and were milder in immunocompromized patients than in healthy subjects and were predictive for a positive vaccination outcome for individual subjects. CONCLUSIONS: Intradermal influenza vaccination is a feasible alternative for standard intramuscular vaccination in several groups of immunocompromized patients, including those treated with anti-TNF, HIV-infected patients and HSCT patients. The occurrence of a local skin reaction after intradermal vaccination is predictive of a response to at least one of the vaccine antigens.

Title: Safety and efficacy of a novel microneedle device for dose sparing intradermal influenza vaccination in healthy adults
Authors: Van Damme, Pierre; Oosterhuis-Kafeja, Froukje; Van der Wielen, Marie; Almagor, Yotam; Sharon, Ofer; Levin, Yotam
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): 6 μg and 3 μg HA/strain intradermal influenza vaccine delivered with MicronJet microneedle device
Schedule / timing: Single vaccination; immunogenicity assessed 21 days after injection
Primary endpoint: Safety and immunogenicity of low-dose ID influenza vaccination delivered by microneedle
Immunogenicity endpoint: GMT fold increase and satisfaction of CPMP relicensure criteria
Safety endpoint: Local and systemic reactions; device reliability
NI result: Reduced-dose ID arms produced immunogenic responses similar to full-dose IM by abstract-level reporting
Key numerical result(s): 180 healthy adults were randomized. Participants received IM 15 μg/strain, ID 6 μg/strain, or ID 3 μg/strain. GMT fold rises at day 21 were 22, 18, and 22 for H1N1; 9, 9, and 16 for H3N2; and 9, 13, and 11 for B in the ID1, ID2, and IM groups, respectively. CPMP relicensure criteria were met in all groups.
Reason not meta-analyzable: Three-arm device trial with abstract-level GMT summaries but no full arm-level seroprotection tables.
Data extraction notes: Abstract/web only. Important microneedle-device antigen-sparing influenza study; full text would help with per-arm seroconversion and safety details.
Screening note: Primary dose-sparing intradermal influenza device trial
TL;DR / study gist: This trial tested whether a microneedle device could deliver much smaller intradermal influenza doses without sacrificing immunogenicity. Both 3 μg and 6 μg intradermal regimens performed broadly similarly to full-dose intramuscular vaccination, with more mild local reactions but acceptable safety.
Original abstract: BACKGROUND: Intradermal vaccine delivery has been shown to induce good immune responses with low vaccine doses. Technologies for drug-delivery which specifically target the skin may render intradermal vaccination more accessible. METHODS: We conducted a prospective, randomized trial in 180 intended-to-treat healthy adults. Study objectives were to evaluate the safety and immunogenicity of low-dose intradermal (ID) influenza vaccines delivered using a novel microneedle device (MicronJet). This device replaces a conventional needle, and is designed specifically for intradermal delivery. Subjects were randomly assigned to receive either the full-dose standard flu shot (containing 15 microg hemagglutinin per strain) delivered intramuscularly using a conventional needle (IM group), a medium dose intradermal injection (6 microg hemagglutinin per strain) delivered with the MicronJet (ID2 group), or a low-dose intradermal injection (3 microg hemagglutinin per strain) delivered with the MicronJet (ID1 group). A marketed influenza vaccine for the 2006/2007 influenza season (alpha-RIX by GSK Biologicals) was used for all injections. Adverse events were recorded over a 42-day period. Immunogenicity was evaluated by changes in hemagglutination inhibition (HAI) antibody titer, and by comparing geometric mean titers (GMTs), seroconversion, and seroprotection rates between the study groups. RESULTS: Local reactions were significantly more frequent following intradermal vaccination, but were mild and transient in nature. At 21 days after injection, GMT fold increase was 22, 18 and 22 in the ID1, ID2 and IM groups respectively for the H1N1 strain; 9, 9 and 16 for the H3N2 strain and 9, 13 and 11 for strain B. The CPMP criteria for re-licensure of seasonal influenza vaccines were met in full for all study groups. CONCLUSIONS: Low-dose influenza vaccines delivered intradermally using microneedles elicited immunogenic responses similar to those elicited by the full-dose intramuscular vaccination. The microneedle injection device used in this study was found to be effective, safe, and reliable.

Title: Half- vs full-dose trivalent inactivated influenza vaccine (2004-2005): age, dose, and sex effects on immune responses
Authors: Engler, Renata J. M.; Nelson, Michael R.; Klote, Mary M.; VanRaden, Mark J.; Huang, Chiung-Yu; Cox, Nancy J.; Klimov, Alexander; Keitel, Wendy A.; Nichol, Kristin L.; Carr, Warner W.; Treanor, John J.; Walter Reed Health Care System Influenza Vaccine Consortium
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): Half-dose intramuscular TIV
Schedule / timing: Single 2004/2005 seasonal vaccination; HAI before and 21 days after vaccination
Primary endpoint: Non-inferiority of half-dose versus full-dose TIV based on proportion achieving HAI titer ≥1:40
Immunogenicity endpoint: Proportion with strain-specific HAI titer ≥1:40 and GMTs
Safety endpoint: Injection-site and systemic reactions; influenza-like illness visits/hospitalizations
Non-inferiority margin: Difference of less than 20% in the upper 95% CI for the proportion with HAI titer ≥1:40
NI result: Generally non-inferior in healthy previously immunized adults, especially ages 18–49
Key numerical result(s): Among previously immunized adults (N=1114), half-dose vs full-dose TIV was not substantially inferior for all antigens in ages 18–49 and for influenza B and A/H3N2 in ages 50–64. Women had higher GMT responses than men across strains and doses.
Reason not meta-analyzable: Abstract/web provides partial subgroup outcomes but not full arm-level tables by age, sex, and strain.
Data extraction notes: Abstract/web only. Useful non-inferiority influenza shortage study with sex and age subgroup effects; full text needed for detailed extraction.
Screening note: Primary half-dose influenza comparison
TL;DR / study gist: In healthy, previously immunized adults, intramuscular half-dose influenza vaccine generally performed similarly to full dose, particularly in those under 50 years. The study also highlighted stronger antibody responses in women, suggesting that age and sex may matter when considering dose-sparing policies.
Original abstract: BACKGROUND: Optimal public health strategies for managing influenza vaccine shortages are not yet defined. Our objective was to determine the effects of age, sex, and dose on the immunogenicity of intramuscular trivalent inactivated vaccine (TIV). METHODS: Healthy adults aged 18 to 64 years, stratified by age (18-49 and 50-64 years) and sex, were randomized to receive full- or half-dose TIV. Hemagglutination inhibition titers against vaccine antigens were measured before and 21 days after immunization. A primary outcome of noninferiority was defined as a difference of less than 20% in the upper 95% confidence interval (CI) of the proportion of subjects with strain-specific hemagglutination inhibition antibody titers of 1:40 or higher after vaccination. Secondary outcomes included geometric mean titers, after vaccination side effects, and occurrences of influenza-like illnesses. RESULTS: Among previously immunized subjects (N = 1114) receiving half- vs full-dose TIV (age, 18-49 years, n = 284 [half] and n = 274 [full]; and age 50-64 years, n = 276 [half] and n = 280 [full]), CIs for proportions of subjects with hemagglutination inhibition antibody titers of 1:40 or higher excluded substantial reduction for all antigens in the 18- to 49-year age group and for B/Shanghai/361/2002 (B) and A/Fujian/411/2002 (A/H3N2) in the 50- to 64-year age group. Geometric mean titer in the female 18- to 49-year age group exceeded male responses for all strains: responses to half-dose TIV that were comparable with male full-dose responses for A/New Caledonia/20/99 (A/H1N1) antigen, 25.4 (95% CI, 20.9-30.9) vs 25.6 (95% CI, 21.3-30.9); A/H3N2 antigen, 60.8 (95% CI, 50.8-72.7) vs 44.1 (95% CI, 37.6-51.8); and B antigen, 64.4 (95% CI, 53.9-76.9) vs 60.7 (95% CI, 51.4-71.7) (findings were similar for the 50- to 64-year age group). Some injection site and systemic reactions (myalgias and/or arthralgias [P < .05], headache [P < .001], and impact of fatigue [P < .05]) were significantly lower in men. The relative risk of medical visits and hospitalizations for influenza-like illnesses were similar in the half- and full-dose groups regardless of age. CONCLUSIONS: Antibody responses to intramuscular half-dose TIV in healthy, previously immunized adults were not substantially inferior to the full-dose vaccine, particularly for ages 18 to 49 years. Significantly higher geometric mean titer responses in women were identified for all ages, regardless of dose or influenza strain. Half-dose vaccination may be an effective strategy for healthy adults younger than 50 years in the setting of an influenza vaccine shortage.

Title: Immunogenicity of Fractional Doses of Tetravalent A/C/Y/W135 Meningococcal Polysaccharide Vaccine: Results from a Randomized Non-Inferiority Controlled Trial in Uganda
Authors: Guerin, Philippe J.; Næss, Lisbeth M.; Fogg, Carole; Rosenqvist, Einar; Pinoges, Loretxu; Bajunirwe, Francis; Nabasumba, Carolyn; Borrow, Ray; Frøholm, Leif O.; Ghabri, Salah; Batwala, Vincent; Twesigye, Rogers; Aaberge, Ingeborg S.; Røttingen, John-Arne; Piola, Patrice; Caugant, Dominique A.
Comparator (full dose / standard / other: Full-dose tetravalent A/C/Y/W135 meningococcal polysaccharide vaccine (50 μg/component)
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): 1/5 dose (10 μg/component) and 1/10 dose (5 μg/component)
Schedule / timing: Single vaccination; safety followed for 4 weeks; immunogenicity at 4 weeks
Sample size analyzed: MITT analyses reported; exact analyzed n not clearly summarized in snippet
Primary endpoint: Responder proportion at 4 weeks based on SBA criteria
Immunogenicity endpoint: Serum bactericidal activity responders (≥4-fold increase and titer ≥128); secondary ELISA IgG response
Safety endpoint: Safety and tolerability over 4 weeks
NI result: 1/5 dose was non-inferior for serogroups W135 and Y, and for serogroup A only in the non-immune population; non-inferiority not shown for serogroup C. 1/10 dose also failed for serogroup A/C overall.
Key numerical result(s): 750 volunteers aged 2–19 years were randomized: 291 full dose, 225 1/5 dose, and 234 1/10 dose. For W135, responder rates were 94% (1/5), 97% (1/10), and 94% (full dose); for A they were 92%, 88%, and 95%, respectively. Non-inferiority was demonstrated for W135 and Y against both fractional doses in total-population analysis, but not for C.
Data extraction notes: Open-access PLOS paper with usable trial details. Interpretation is serogroup-specific rather than a single yes/no overall NI result.
Screening note: Primary non-inferiority trial of fractional meningococcal vaccine
TL;DR / study gist: In Ugandan participants aged 2–19 years, a 1/5 dose of tetravalent meningococcal polysaccharide vaccine preserved bactericidal responses for serogroups W135 and Y, but not consistently for C. The results suggest fractional dosing could help in outbreaks during shortages, but performance depended on serogroup and baseline immunity.
Original abstract: Meningitis are infections of the lining of the brain and spinal cord and can cause high fever, blood poisoning, and brain damage, as well as result in death in up to 10% of cases. Epidemics of meningitis occur almost every year in parts of sub-Saharan Africa, throughout a high-burden area spanning Senegal to Ethiopia dubbed the “Meningitis Belt.” Most epidemics in Africa are caused by Neisseria meningitidis (mostly serogroup A and W135). Mass vaccination campaigns attempt to control epidemics by administering meningococcal vaccines targeted against these serogroups, among others. However, global shortages of these vaccines are currently seen. We studied the use of fractional (1/5 and 1/10) doses of a licensed vaccine to assess its non-inferiority compared with the normal full dose. In a randomized trial in Uganda, we found that immune response and safety using a 1/5 dose were comparable to full dose for three serogroups (A, Y, W135), though not a fourth (C). In light of current shortages of meningococcal vaccines and their importance in fighting meningitis epidemics around the world, we suggest fractional doses be taken under consideration in mass vaccination campaigns.

Title: Intradermally Administered Yellow Fever Vaccine at Reduced Dose Induces a Protective Immune Response: A Randomized Controlled Non-Inferiority Trial
Authors: Roukens, Anna H.; Vossen, Ann C.; Bredenbeek, Peter J.; Dissel, Jaap T. van; Visser, Leo G.
Comparator (full dose / standard / other: Standard 0.5 mL subcutaneous yellow fever 17D vaccine
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): Reduced-dose 0.1 mL intradermal yellow fever 17D vaccine (1/5 dose)
Schedule / timing: Single vaccination; neutralization titers measured from 2 weeks to 1 year
Primary endpoint: Protective seroimmunity/non-inferiority of ID fractional dose versus standard SC dose
Immunogenicity endpoint: Neutralizing antibody titers and WHO seroprotection threshold
Safety endpoint: Adverse events diary and viraemia
Non-inferiority margin: Not clearly stated in available abstract lines
NI result: Non-inferior; all primary vaccinees in both groups reached protective neutralization thresholds
Key numerical result(s): A total of 155 primary vaccinees and 20 previously vaccinated volunteers participated. From 2 weeks to 1 year, protective neutralization did not differ between reduced-dose ID and standard-dose SC groups, and WHO-standard seroprotection was achieved in 77/77 and 78/78 primary vaccinees, respectively.
Data extraction notes: Strong early fractional yellow fever trial. Full text would be useful for exact NI margin and quantitative GMTs, but headline efficacy-style immunogenicity results are clear.
Screening note: Primary human study of fractional/reduced-dose vaccine regimen
TL;DR / study gist: This Dutch trial showed that one-fifth-dose intradermal yellow fever vaccine generated protective neutralizing responses that matched standard subcutaneous vaccination over one year. It is one of the clearest early proofs of concept that fractional yellow fever dosing can work.
Original abstract: BackgroundImplementation of yellow fever vaccination is currently hampered by limited supply of vaccine. An alternative route of administration with reduced amounts of vaccine but without loss of vaccine efficacy would boost vaccination programmes.Methods and FindingsA randomized, controlled, non-inferiority trial was conducted in a Dutch university center between August 2005 and February 2007. A total of 155 primary vaccinated and 20 previously vaccinated volunteers participated. Participants were randomly assigned in a 1∶1 ratio to receive intradermal (i.d.) vaccination with live attenuated yellow fever 17D vaccine at a reduced dose (1/5th; 0·1 mL) or the conventional subcutaneous (s.c.) vaccination (0·5 mL). Antibody neutralization titers were determined at 2, 4 and 8 weeks and 1 year after vaccination by counting the reduction in virus-induced plaques in the presence of serial serum dilutions. Adverse events were documented in a 3-week dairy. Viraemia was measured 5 days after vaccination. From 2 weeks up to one year after vaccination, the maximum serum-dilution at which 80% of the virus plaques were neutralized, which indicates protection against yellow fever, did not differ between those given a reduced i.d. dose or standard s.c. dose of vaccine. In all cases the WHO standard of seroprotection (i.e. 80% virus neutralization) was reached (in 77/77 and 78/78, respectively). Similar results were found in the previously vaccinated individuals. Viraemia was detected in half of the primary vaccinated participants, which was not predictive of serological response. In revaccinees no viraemia was detected.ConclusionsIntradermal administration of one fifth of the amount of yellow fever vaccine administered subcutaneously results in protective seroimmunity in all volunteers. Albeit this vaccination route should enable vaccination of five-times as many individuals at risk for disease, these results should now be confirmed in field studies in areas with potential yellow fever virus transmission to change vaccination policy.Trial RegistrationNederlands Trial Register ISRCTN46326316

Title: Antibody responses after dose-sparing intradermal influenza vaccination
Authors: Auewarakul, Prasert; Kositanont, Uraiwan; Sornsathapornkul, Pornchai; Tothong, Paichit; Kanyok, Raweewan; Thongcharoen, Prasert
Comparator (full dose / standard / other: 0.5 mL intramuscular inactivated split-virion influenza vaccine
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): 0.1 mL intradermal influenza vaccine (1/5 dose)
Schedule / timing: Single vaccination; HAI assessed at day 28
Primary endpoint: Comparative antibody response after ID 1/5 dose versus full-dose IM vaccination
Immunogenicity endpoint: HAI titers ≥1:40 and GMT fold increase for A(H1N1), A(H3N2), and B
Safety endpoint: Local reactions
NI result: Inferior on immunogenicity versus full-dose IM, though responses still met CPMP relicensure criteria
Key numerical result(s): 500 subjects were randomized. Day-28 HAI titers ≥1:40 in ID vs IM groups were 93.3% vs 98.0% for H1N1, 86.3% vs 95.0% for H3N2, and 43.5% vs 57.0% for B. GMT fold increases in ID vs IM groups were 16 vs 31 (H1N1), 8 vs 20 (H3N2), and 2 vs 3 (B).
Data extraction notes: Abstract provides several usable comparative immunogenicity values. Would still want full text for denominators and any age/subgroup details.
Screening note: Primary intradermal dose-sparing influenza study
TL;DR / study gist: A Thai randomized trial found that one-fifth-dose intradermal influenza vaccination generated weaker antibody responses than standard intramuscular vaccination, especially for H3N2 and B. Even so, the reduced-dose strategy still reached licensing-style immunogenicity thresholds, making it a plausible shortage strategy rather than a true equivalent.
Original abstract: Reduced-dose intradermal (ID) influenza vaccination is an attractive approach to increase availability of vaccine supply in an event of vaccine shortage. We conducted a randomized open-label study, in which 500 subjects were randomly assigned to receive an ID injection of 0.1 ml dose of inactivated split-virion influenza vaccine or an IM injection of 0.5 ml dose.
