The subjects who had hemagglutination inhibition (HI) antibody titer of at least 1:40 at day 28 post-vaccination in ID and IM groups were 93.3% versus 98.0% for influenza A(H1N1) virus, 86.3% versus 95.0% for A(H3N2) virus, and 43.5% versus 57.0% for influenza B virus. Subjects in the ID group had an increase in geometric mean titer by a factor of 16 for the H1N1 strain, 8 for the H3N2 strain, and 2 for the B strain on day 28, as compared with respective increase in the IM group of 31, 20, and 3. Local reactions were significantly more frequent among subjects in the ID group than those in the IM group, but the reactions were mild and transient. In this study, ID administration of one-fifth dose of influenza vaccine elicited significantly lower levels of antibody response as compared to full-dose IM injection. However, the antibody responses elicited by the ID vaccination were still sufficiently high to meet the requirement guidelines of the European Committee for Proprietary Medicinal Products (CPMP) for the annual relicensure of influenza vaccines.

Title: Immunogenicity and safety of intradermal influenza immunization at a reduced dose in healthy children
Authors: Chiu, Susan S.; Peiris, J. S. Malik; Chan, Kwok H.; Wong, Wilfred Hing Sang; Lau, Yu Lung
Outcome domains: Immunogenicity; Safety; Clinical efficacy/effectiveness; Follow-up / persistence
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): One-fifth-dose intradermal influenza vaccine
Schedule / timing: Single vaccination; immunogenicity assessed 21 days after vaccination
Primary endpoint: Whether one-fifth-dose ID vaccination in children elicits immunogenicity comparable to full-dose IM vaccination
Immunogenicity endpoint: GMTs, fold increase, seroprotection, and seroconversion for vaccine strains
Safety endpoint: Injection-site reactions and general safety
NI result: Comparable by abstract-level reporting
Key numerical result(s): 112 healthy children aged 3 to <18 years were randomized. Local induration and mild erythema occurred in 25% and 57% of ID recipients, respectively. Fold rise in GMTs was similar for A/Caledonia and B/Shandong, and both groups generated high GMTs against A/Panama; the abstract concludes ID one-fifth dosing was comparable to standard IM vaccination.
Reason not meta-analyzable: Abstract/web gives only selected GMT summaries and not complete arm-level response tables.
Data extraction notes: Abstract/web only. Good pediatric intradermal influenza trial; full text needed for complete strain-specific endpoints and exact group denominators.
Screening note: Primary reduced-dose intradermal influenza study in children
TL;DR / study gist: In healthy children aged 3 years and older, one-fifth-dose intradermal influenza vaccination produced broadly comparable immunogenicity to standard intramuscular vaccination. The trade-off was more local skin reactions, but no major safety issues were flagged in the abstract.
Original abstract: OBJECTIVES: We conducted this study to test the hypothesis that intradermal influenza vaccination at one fifth of a standard dose elicits comparable immunogenicity to full-dose intramuscular vaccination in children. PATIENTS AND METHODS: We conducted a randomized, open-label study in 112 healthy children aged 3 to <18 years to compare the immunogenicity and safety of intradermal vaccination at one fifth of a dose with standard intramuscular vaccination. Analyses of hemagglutination inhibition antibody titers to each antigen in each group included geometric mean titers before and 21 days after vaccination, fold increase in geometric mean titers after vaccination, seroprotection rate, and seroconversion rate. RESULTS: The mean age of the subjects was 10.11 +/- 4.04 years in the intradermal vaccination group and 10.57 +/- 3.91 years in the intramuscular group. Intradermal vaccination was safe. Induration and mild erythema at the injection site were reported at 25% and 57%, respectively, in the intradermal group. Fold increase of geometric mean titers against influenza A/Caledonia was robust in both groups (11.1-fold and 12.9-fold increase in the intramuscular and intradermal groups, respectively), whereas that for B/Shandong was more modest (4.3-4.4). Both approaches elicited very high geometric mean titers against influenza A/Panama: 1360.5 and 893.9 for the intramuscular and intradermal groups, respectively, but because the prevaccination antibody titers were high, the fold increase of geometric mean titers was only 4.5 and 2.6, respectively. CONCLUSION: The immunogenicity of one fifth of a dose of influenza vaccine delivered by the intradermal route is comparable to the standard-dose intramuscular vaccination in children as young as 3 years of age.

Title: Effectiveness of half-dose versus full-dose influenza vaccine in health care workers
Authors: Kramer, Joan S.; Durham, Chris; Schroeder, Todd; Garrelts, James C.
Journal / source: American journal of health-system pharmacy: AJHP: official journal of the American Society of Health-System Pharmacists
Country / setting: United States; tertiary care community hospital
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): Half-dose TIV, 0.25 mL intramuscular
Schedule / timing: Single vaccination; symptom questionnaires monthly from Nov 2004 to Mar 2005
Sample size analyzed: 444 randomized; exact analyzed denominator not clearly stated
Primary endpoint: Incidence of influenza between half-dose and full-dose groups
Immunogenicity endpoint: Not assessed in abstract
Clinical endpoint: Clinical diagnosis of influenza; work days missed; self-reported signs/symptoms
Safety endpoint: Not a major focus in abstract
NI result: No statistically significant difference in effectiveness; not a formal NI analysis
Key numerical result(s): 444 health care workers were randomized (222 per arm). Influenza diagnosis occurred in 4% of full-dose recipients versus 7% of half-dose recipients (p=0.198). Only 1 laboratory-confirmed influenza case occurred, in the full-dose arm.
Reason not meta-analyzable: Clinical outcome based mainly on self-report/clinical diagnosis with minimal laboratory confirmation.
Data extraction notes: Useful as a pragmatic shortage-strategy study, but not ideal for pooled immunogenicity meta-analysis.
Screening note: Primary half-dose vs full-dose influenza effectiveness study
Abstract: PURPOSE: A prospective, randomized, double-blind study comparing the effectiveness of half-dose versus full-dose trivalent inactivated influenza vaccine (TIV) in health care workers was conducted. METHODS: The study was conducted at a 760-bed tertiary care community hospital. Study participants (n = 444) were hospital staff or employees over 18 years of age who were enrolled during a two-week period in October and November 2004. Participants were assigned to either full-dose (n = 222) or half-dose (n = 222) TIV. The participants completed a standardized questionnaire monthly from November 2004 through March 2005. The primary outcome was the incidence of influenza between the two groups; the secondary outcomes were self-reported days of work missed as a result of influenza and the proportion of participants in each group self-reporting signs and symptoms of influenza. Laboratory confirmation of influenza diagnosis was sought in participants reporting a clinical diagnosis by their physicians. Differences in the groups were analyzed with Fisher's exact test, with a < 0.05 considered significant. RESULTS: There was no difference between the full-dose and half-dose groups in the diagnosis of influenza (4% versus 7%, p = 0.198). Laboratory confirmation of an influenza diagnosis occurred in one participant who received full-dose TIV. CONCLUSION: Half-dose TIV demonstrated a similar effectiveness to full-dose TIV, measured by the number of diagnoses of influenza and symptom survey of participating health care workers. Administration of half-dose TIV is an acceptable strategy for maximizing the number of vaccinations in health care workers, but additional studies are needed to confirm the comparable immunogenicity and effectiveness between the two doses.
TL;DR / study gist: In health care workers, half-dose intramuscular influenza vaccine performed similarly to full-dose vaccine for clinically diagnosed influenza over one season. The study supports half-dosing as a shortage strategy, though laboratory confirmation was sparse and immunogenicity was not measured.

Title: Low-dose intradermal and subcutaneous versus intramuscular hepatitis B vaccination in primary non-responding hemodialysis patients
Authors: Sorkhi, Hadi; Dooki, Mohammad Reza Esmaeli; Ebrahimnejad, Mina Sadat
Journal / source: Journal of the Medical Association of Thailand = Chotmaihet Thangphaet
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): Fourth-dose 10 microg intradermal booster and 10 microg subcutaneous booster
Schedule / timing: After prior 3-dose IM series; fourth booster assessed at 45 days and 6 months
Primary endpoint: Anti-HBs response after booster by route and dose
Immunogenicity endpoint: Anti-HBs titer >100 mIU/mL at 45 days and 6 months
Safety endpoint: Not reported in abstract
NI result: No significant difference between groups, but not a formal NI analysis
Key numerical result(s): 34 hemodialysis nonresponders were randomized. At 45 days, anti-HBs >100 mIU/mL was seen in 6/11 IM, 4/12 ID, and 3/11 SC recipients; at 6 months, 4/11 IM, 2/12 ID, and 3/11 SC remained above 100 mIU/mL.
Reason not meta-analyzable: Three-arm booster study in prior nonresponders with small sample size and threshold-based reporting only.
Data extraction notes: Relevant reduced-dose hepatitis B booster comparison; exact safety/reactogenicity details need full text.
Screening note: Primary low-dose intradermal/subcutaneous vs intramuscular hepatitis B study
Abstract: OBJECTIVE: Patients with end-stage renal failure are at high risk of hepatitis B virus (HB V) infection. They have impaired immune response to HBV intramuscular (i.m.) vaccine. Non-response (anti HBs titer < 100mIU/ml) hemodialysis patients (HD) with the previous three-dose i.m. vaccination were examined with booster dose vaccine by i.m. , intradermal (i.d) and subcutaneous (s.c.) routes. MATERIAL AND METHOD: Thirty-four HD patients who had been vaccinated with three-dose vaccine (40 microgram, 2 ml, Engerix B, i. m.) and had anti-HBs titer less than 100mlU/ml were selected. They were randomly divided into three groups and received a fourth dose of vaccine by i.m. (40 microgram, 2 ml), i.d (10 microgram. 0. 5 ml) and s.c. (10 microgram, 0. 5 ml). Then, serum anti-HBs titer was determined after 45 days and 6 months. RESULTS: Forty five days after completion of the re-vaccination course, anti-HBs titer was above 100 mIU/ml in 6/11, 3/11 and 4/12 of i.m. s.c. and i. d groups, respectively (p > 0.05). After six months, 4/11, 3/11 and 2/12 of patients had anti-HBs titer above l00mlU/ml (p > 0.05). CONCLUSION: With lower dose of vaccine (10 microgram) in s.c. groups, these patients had lower change in their anti-HBs titer. Therefore, it is cost effective and practical to offer other vaccination schemes.
TL;DR / study gist: Among hemodialysis patients who had failed a prior hepatitis B vaccine series, a reduced-dose intradermal booster performed broadly similarly to a full-dose intramuscular booster, while reduced-dose subcutaneous dosing looked less attractive. The study is small but suggests intradermal dose-sparing may be reasonable in this hard-to-immunize group.

Title: Low dose intradermal versus high dose intramuscular hepatitis B vaccination in patients on chronic hemodialysis
Authors: Roozbeh, Jamshid; Moini, Maryam; Lankarani, Kamran Bagheri; Sagheb, Mohamad Medhi; Shahpoori, Sedigheh; Bastani, Bahar
Comparator (full dose / standard / other: 40 microg intramuscular vaccine at 0, 1, and 4 months
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): 20 microg intradermal vaccine at 0, 1, and 4 months
Schedule / timing: Three-dose series at 0, 1, and 4 months; assessed 1 and 6 months after last dose
Primary endpoint: HBs-Ab seroconversion after low-dose ID versus high-dose IM vaccination
Immunogenicity endpoint: Seroconversion rate (HBs-Ab >10 IU/L) and antibody titer by ELISA
Safety endpoint: Not reported in abstract
NI result: Similar seroconversion between groups; not a formal NI analysis
Key numerical result(s): 86 new HD patients were randomized. At 1 month after dose 3, seroconversion was 55.6% in the 40 microg IM group and 54.3% in the 20 microg ID group; at 6 months, both groups were 50.0%.
Data extraction notes: Usable comparative response proportions are reported, though denominator loss by follow-up should be checked in full text.
Screening note: Primary low-dose intradermal vs high-dose IM hepatitis B study
Abstract: Patients with end-stage renal disease on hemodialysis (HD) are at high risk for hepatitis B infection. We randomly assigned 86 new patients on HD to receive either 40 microg intramuscular (group 1) or 20 microg intradermal (group 2) recombinant hepatitis B vaccine, in three doses at 0, 1, and 4 months. All patients were seronegative at baseline for hepatitis B surface antigen (HBs-Ag), hepatitis B surface antibody (HBs-Ab), and hepatitis B core antibody (HBc-Ab). HBs-Ab seroconversion rate and antibody titer (ELISA assay) were compared in 27 patients of group 1 and 35 patients of group 2 at 1 month, and in 20 patients of group 1 and 26 patients of group 2 at 6 months after the last vaccine dose. The seroconversion rates (HBs-Ab titer >10 IU/L) were 55.6% and 50% in group 1, and 54.3% and 50% in group 2, at 1 and 6 months, respectively (p = NS). Patients' age, body mass index, serum albumin concentration, and sex distribution were similar in the responders and nonresponders. Intradermal hepatitis B vaccination used at half dose may be a cost saving alternative to intramuscular vaccination in patients on HD. However, the low overall seroconversion rate mandates seeking alternative ways of vaccination in this patient population.
TL;DR / study gist: This hemodialysis trial found that a half-dose intradermal hepatitis B schedule produced almost identical seroconversion rates to a higher-dose intramuscular schedule, although overall response rates remained modest in both groups. It is a useful cost-saving comparison in a difficult population.

Title: Dose Sparing with Intradermal Injection of Influenza Vaccine
Authors: Kenney, Richard T.; Frech, Sarah A.; Muenz, Larry R.; Villar, Christina P.; Glenn, Gregory M.
Comparator (full dose / standard / other: Standard-dose IM trivalent influenza vaccine, 0.5 mL with at least 15 microg HA/strain
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): One-fifth-dose ID trivalent influenza vaccine, 0.1 mL with at least 3 microg HA/strain
Schedule / timing: Single vaccination; day-21 antibody and reactogenicity assessment
Primary endpoint: Comparison of HAI antibody responses and safety after ID reduced-dose versus IM standard-dose vaccination
Immunogenicity endpoint: GMT fold rise, seroconversion, and seroprotection by HAI assay
Safety endpoint: Local and systemic adverse events
NI result: Comparable by abstract-level reporting; no formal NI margin reported
Key numerical result(s): 100 healthy adults were randomized. ID one-fifth-dose recipients had day-21 GMT fold increases of 15.2 (H1N1), 19.0 (H3N2), and 12.4 (B) versus 14.9, 7.1, and 15.3 after full-dose IM vaccination. Seroconversion ranged 66-82% and seroprotection 84-100% in both groups.
Data extraction notes: Classic influenza dose-sparing trial; good comparative immunogenicity summary in abstract.
Screening note: Primary human influenza study of reduced-dose intradermal vs standard intramuscular vaccination in COPD patients.
Abstract: In this trial, 50 healthy adults 18 to 40 years of age received an intradermal injection of one fifth the standard dose of an influenza vaccine. The resulting increases in hemagglutination-inhibition titers were at least similar to those elicited by the standard dose of vaccine administered intramuscularly to 50 control subjects. 50 healthy adults received an intradermal injection of one fifth the standard dose of an influenza vaccine. The use of reduced doses of vaccine may be one way of expanding a limited supply of vaccine. Vaccination against influenzavirus is an important public health measure to help protect against the annual morbidity and mortality associated with influenza. A major global supplier of influenza vaccine recently experienced manufacturing problems that led regulatory authorities to declare more than 48 million doses, almost half the U.S. supply of influenza vaccine, unfit for use.1 This has created a public health crisis, with widespread shortages leaving many in the United States without vaccine coverage. Various emergency measures are being considered in an effort to expand the supply and consist either of reducing the intramuscular dose or importing doses from manufacturers that . . .
TL;DR / study gist: A one-fifth intradermal dose of influenza vaccine produced antibody responses very similar to standard full-dose intramuscular vaccination in healthy young adults. Local skin reactions were more common with intradermal delivery, but they were mild and short-lived.

Title: Dose response of CRM197 and tetanus toxoid-conjugated Haemophilus influenzae type b vaccines
Authors: Huebner, Robin E.; Nicol, Mark; Mothupi, Rosalia; Käyhty, Helena; Mbelle, Nontombi; Khomo, Esther; Klugman, Keith P.
Vaccine / intervention: CRM197-conjugated and tetanus toxoid-conjugated Hib vaccines
Comparator (full dose / standard / other: Higher-dose Hib conjugate vaccine arms up to 10 microg
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): 0.625, 1.25, 2.5, and 5.0 microg dose arms
Schedule / timing: Primary series at 6, 10, and 14 weeks; booster at 9 months
Primary endpoint: Anti-PRP antibody response across Hib conjugate dose levels
Immunogenicity endpoint: Anti-PRP IgG concentration at 18 weeks and one week after booster; proportion above 0.15 and 1.0 microg/mL
Safety endpoint: Not reported in abstract
NI result: Doses >=1.25 microg appeared sufficient; no clear dose-response advantage above that level
Key numerical result(s): For both CRM197 and tetanus-toxoid conjugates, doses >=1.25 microg achieved >0.15 microg/mL in at least 98-100% of children and >1.0 microg/mL in at least 79-88.9%. Booster responses rose to >=37.71 microg/mL for HibCRM197 and >=16.07 microg/mL for Hib-tetanus at doses >=1.25 microg.
Reason not meta-analyzable: Randomized dose-ranging design with multiple arms but abstract lacks denominators and full arm-level counts.
Data extraction notes: Useful evidence that Hib fractional doses can retain strong immunogenicity; full text needed for arm sizes and safety.
Screening note: Human Hib dose-response trial undertaken to identify lower immunogenic doses for cost reduction.
Abstract: High vaccine cost has limited use of conjugate vaccines in the developing world where the disease burden is greatest. Fixed fractional doses of Haemophilus influenzae type b (Hib) vaccines have been shown to be immunogenic, but dose responses of these vaccines in humans are needed to determine the lowest immunogenic dose as an option for lowering vaccine cost. We randomized children to receive one of five doses (0.625, 1.25, 2.5, 5.0 and 10 microg) of either a diphtheria CRM197 or tetanus toxoid-conjugated Hib vaccine. The children received a primary three-dose series at 6, 10, and 14 weeks of age and a booster dose at 9 months. Anti-PRP IgG antibodies were measured at each vaccination, at 18 weeks, and at one week following the booster dose. Concentrations of > or =1.25 microg of HibCRM197 vaccine produced mean anti-PRP responses at 18 weeks of > or =5.72 microg/ml and > or =0.15 microg/ml was achieved in >98% of the children with at least 79% reaching anti-PRP concentrations of > or =1.0 microg/ml. Concentrations of > or =1.25 microg of Hib-tetanus vaccine produced mean anti-PRP responses at 18 weeks of > or =8.63 microg/ml and > or =0.15 microg/ml was achieved in 100% of the children with at least 88.9% reaching anti-PRP concentrations of > or =1.0 microg/ml. While mean antibody concentrations after either vaccine decreased over time, the proportion of children with antibody levels of > or =0.15 microg/ml had not changed significantly at the 9 month measurement. Immunologic memory was demonstrated by significant increases in mean antibody concentrations one week after the booster dose for doses > or =1.25 microg of HibCRM197 and Hib-tetanus to mean concentrations > or =37.71 and 16.07 microg/ml, respectively. There were no differences in antibody responses for vaccine doses > or =1.25 microg of the same vaccine or between the same concentrations of the two different vaccines. Our data suggest that doses of these vaccines of > or =1.25 microg may be sufficient to stimulate an immune response that offers both short and longer term protection from invasive Hib disease.
TL;DR / study gist: This dose-ranging Hib study suggests that reducing the conjugate dose down to around 1.25 microg still preserved strong primary and booster antibody responses. In other words, doses above that threshold did not seem to buy much extra immunogenicity.

We randomized children to receive one of five doses (0.625, 1.25, 2.5, 5.0 and 10 microg) of either a diphtheria CRM197 or tetanus toxoid-conjugated Hib vaccine. The children received a primary three-dose series at 6, 10, and 14 weeks of age and a booster dose at 9 months. Anti-PRP IgG antibodies were measured at each vaccination, at 18 weeks, and at one week following the booster dose. Concentrations of > or =1.25 microg of HibCRM197 vaccine produced mean anti-PRP responses at 18 weeks of > or =5.72 microg/ml and > or =0.15 microg/ml was achieved in >98% of the children with at least 79% reaching anti-PRP concentrations of > or =1.0 microg/ml. Concentrations of > or =1.25 microg of Hib-tetanus vaccine produced mean anti-PRP responses at 18 weeks of > or =8.63 microg/ml and > or =0.15 microg/ml was achieved in 100% of the children with at least 88.9% reaching anti-PRP concentrations of > or =1.0 microg/ml. While mean antibody concentrations after either vaccine decreased over time, the proportion of children with antibody levels of > or =0.15 microg/ml had not changed significantly at the 9 month measurement. There were no differences in antibody responses for vaccine doses > or =1.25 microg of the same vaccine or between the same concentrations of the two different vaccines. Title: Serum antibody responses after intradermal vaccination against influenza
Authors: Belshe, Robert B.; Newman, Frances K.; Cannon, Joan; Duane, Carol; Treanor, John; Van Hoecke, Christian; Howe, Barbara J.; Dubin, Gary
Journal / source: The New England Journal of Medicine
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): 40% intradermal dose, 6 microg HA/antigen
Schedule / timing: Single vaccination; post-vaccination antibody assessment (timing not specified in snippet)
Primary endpoint: Serum antibody response after reduced-dose ID versus standard-dose IM influenza vaccination
Immunogenicity endpoint: HAI titers >=1:40 and comparative antibody responses by age group
Safety endpoint: Not clearly stated in abstract snippet
NI result: Comparable in adults 18-60; trend toward better IM response in >60 years, significant for H3N2 only
Key numerical result(s): 119 participants per arm were randomized. Adults 18-60 years had vigorous responses without significant differences between routes, and all had HAI titers >=1:40. In those >60 years, responses also rose well but favored IM for H3N2.
Data extraction notes: Good comparative influenza ID study stratified by age; full text would give complete strain-specific tables and safety data.
Screening note: Primary human influenza study comparing 40% intradermal dose with standard intramuscular dose.
Abstract: BACKGROUND: If found to be safe and immunogenic, reduced doses of influenza vaccine given by the intradermal route could increase the number of available doses of vaccine. METHODS: In an open-label study, we randomly assigned 119 subjects to receive an intradermal injection of trivalent inactivated influenza vaccine, containing 6 mug of hemagglutinin for each antigen (40 percent of the usual dose), and 119 to receive an intramuscular injection of the standard dose of 15 mug of hemagglutinin for each antigen. The two groups were subdivided according to age (18 to 60 years and older than 60 years). RESULTS: Among subjects who were 18 to 60 years of age, serum antibody responses were vigorous and did not differ significantly between the intradermal and intramuscular groups, and all subjects had hemagglutination-inhibition (HAI) titers of at least 1:40. Although the subjects who were older than 60 years of age also had a vigorous antibody response, there was a trend toward a better response in the intramuscular route, but this finding was significant only for antigen to the H3N2 strain. Nevertheless, 100 percent of older subjects in the intramuscular group and 93 percent of such subjects in the intradermal group had an HAI antibody titer to the H3N2 strain of more than 1:40, and 100 percent in each group had a titer of this level for both the H1N1 and B strains. Local pain was significantly more common in the intramuscular group than in the intradermal group among subjects who were 18 to 60 years of age but not among subjects who were over 60 years old. Signs of local inflammation were significantly more common among subjects in the intradermal group than among those in the intramuscular group, in both age groups. CONCLUSIONS: As compared with an intramuscular injection of full-dose influenza vaccine, an intradermal injection of a reduced dose resulted in similarly vigorous antibody responses among persons 18 to 60 years of age but not among those over the age of 60 years.
TL;DR / study gist: A reduced 40% intradermal influenza dose worked about as well as the full intramuscular dose in adults up to age 60, but older adults may have done slightly better with the standard intramuscular route. This suggests age matters when deciding how far influenza dose-sparing can be pushed.

Title: Immune response of intradermal hepatitis B vaccination at lower dose versus intramuscular vaccination at double standard dose in predialytic chronic renal failure patients
Authors: Somboonsilp, Wanida; Eiam-Ong, Somchai; Tungsanga, Kriang; Tirawatanapong, Thaweesak
Journal / source: Journal of the Medical Association of Thailand = Chotmaihet Thangphaet
Comparator (full dose / standard / other: Double-standard-dose IM vaccine, 20 microg (1.0 mL) at two positions
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): Low-dose ID vaccine, 2 microg total (0.1 mL given at 5 positions)
Schedule / timing: Four vaccinations at months 0, 1, 2, and 6; response assessed at M1, M2, M6, and M7
Primary endpoint: Seroconversion comparison between low-dose ID and double-dose IM hepatitis B vaccination
Immunogenicity endpoint: Anti-HBs seroconversion >10 mIU/mL and distribution of 10-999 vs >=1000 mIU/mL at M7
Safety endpoint: Not reported in abstract
NI result: Comparable satisfactory immune response; not a formal NI analysis
Key numerical result(s): There were no significant differences in seroconversion rates between ID and IM groups at M1, M2, M6, or M7. Among seroconverters at M7, the distribution of good (10-999 mIU/mL) and excellent (>=1000 mIU/mL) responses also did not differ.
Reason not meta-analyzable: Abstract lacks arm sizes and full quantitative response proportions at each time point.
Data extraction notes: Promising predialysis reduced-dose ID hepatitis B comparison; full text needed for sample sizes and exact percentages.
Screening note: Primary lower-dose intradermal hepatitis B comparison
Abstract: The immune responses to hepatitis B vaccine were studied in 2 groups of predialytic chronic renal failure patients who had negative results of hepatitis B surface antigen (HBsAg), hepatitis B surface antibody (anti-HBs) and hepatitis B core antibody (anti HBc). In the intradermal group, vaccine at the dose of 0.1 ml, 2 microg, was intradermally administered at 5 positions. In the intramuscular group, the vaccine at the dose of 1.0 ml, 20 microg, was intramuscularly given at 2 positions and, thus, was a double standard dose. Both groups received 4 vaccinations at month 0, 1, 2, and 6 (M0, M1, M2, and M6). The results showed that there were no significant differences in the results of seroconversion rates, defined as having anti HBs levels above 10 mIU/ml, between the intradermal (ID) and intramuscular (IM) groups at M1, M2, M6, and M7. In patients with positive seroconversion results at M7, the numbers of patients in the good and excellent subgroups, having HBs Ab levels ranged 10-999 and above 1,000 mIU/ml respectively, showed no difference between both routes. The body weight and seroconversion rates at M2 and M6 were the factors which had a positive influence on the seroconversion rates of intradermal hepatitis B vaccination. In conclusion, intradermal hepatitis B vaccination at a lower dose could provide comparable satisfactory immune response with the intramuscular route at double the standard dose.
TL;DR / study gist: In predialysis chronic renal failure patients, a much smaller intradermal hepatitis B dose generated immune responses similar to a double-standard intramuscular dose. It is another piece of evidence that intradermal delivery can partly compensate for lower antigen amounts.

Title: Evaluation of a single dose of half strength inactivated influenza vaccine in healthy adults
Authors: Treanor, John; Keitel, Wendy; Belshe, Robert; Campbell, James; Schiff, Gilbert; Zangwill, Ken; Wolff, Mark; Klimov, Alexander; Levandowski, Roland; Lambert, Linda
Country / setting: United States; six academic medical centers
Comparator (full dose / standard / other: Full-dose IM vaccine, 0.5 mL (15.5 microg HA/strain)
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): Half-dose IM vaccine, 0.25 mL (7.75 microg HA/strain)
Schedule / timing: Single vaccination; HAI assay before and 21 days after vaccination
Primary endpoint: Immunogenicity of half-dose versus full-dose inactivated influenza vaccine
Immunogenicity endpoint: Post-vaccination HAI >=1:40, post-vaccination GMTs, and >=4-fold rises for each strain
Safety endpoint: Not reported in abstract
Non-inferiority margin: Upper 95% confidence limits for differences <20% and GMT ratios <1.5 were considered supportive; no explicit formal NI framing
NI result: Slightly lower responses with half-dose, but differences were small and considered acceptable for shortage conditions
Key numerical result(s): Healthy adults 18-49 years received either 0.5 mL or 0.25 mL IM vaccine. Half-dose recipients had lower rates of HAI >=1:40, lower GMTs, and fewer >=4-fold rises for all three strains, but the upper 95% confidence limits for response differences were all <20%.
Reason not meta-analyzable: Abstract gives comparative direction and confidence bounds but not complete arm-level counts for each strain.
Data extraction notes: Good shortage-strategy influenza trial; full text needed for exact denominators and safety.
Screening note: Primary half-strength influenza trial
Abstract: Because of delays in the manufacturing of the 2000-2001, trivalent inactivated influenza vaccine in the US, there were concerns that there might be shortages of vaccine supply in the US. Therefore, we conducted a prospective, randomized, open-label, multicenter trial at six academic medical centers in the US, to evaluate the immunogenicity of a half dose of inactivated vaccine in healthy adults. Healthy adults between the ages of 18 and 49 were randomized to receive either a full 0.5ml (15.5 microg of each HA antigen) dose or a 0.25ml (7.75 microg of each HA antigen) dose of the 2000-2001 trivalent inactivated influenza vaccine by intramuscular injection. Sera were obtained for assessment of hemagglutination-inhibiting antibody to each of the three strains contained in the vaccine before and 21 days after vaccination. The proportions of individuals achieving a post-vaccination titer of > or =1:40, the geometric mean titers (GMTs) of post-vaccination antibody, and the proportions of individuals with a four-fold or greater increase in antibody were lower for all three strains in those receiving 0.25ml of vaccine compared to those receiving 0.5ml. However, the differences were small for all three antigens. The upper 95% confidence limits for differences between 0.25 and 0.5ml doses were less than 20% for rates of achieving a titer of > or =1:40 and four-fold response, and less than 1.5 for the ratios of GMTs between dose groups, for all three vaccine antigens. These results suggest that when vaccine is in short supply, a strategy using a half dose in healthy adults could increase the number of people vaccinated with relatively little adverse impact on vaccine immunogenicity.
TL;DR / study gist: A half intramuscular dose of seasonal influenza vaccine in healthy adults produced slightly weaker antibody responses than the standard dose, but the drop was modest. The authors argued that in a shortage, vaccinating twice as many healthy adults with half doses could be a reasonable trade-off.

Title: The implication of a reduced-dose hepatitis B vaccination schedule in low risk newborns
Authors: Lee, S. S.; Young, B. W. Y.; Wong, K. H.; Lim, W. L.
Special population: Low-risk newborns born to HBsAg-negative mothers
Study design: Randomized controlled trial with long-term follow-up
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): Reduced-dose 2.5 microg three-dose regimen
Schedule / timing: Three-dose infant schedule; followed for 8 years
Primary endpoint: Long-term implications of regular versus reduced-dose neonatal hepatitis B vaccination
Immunogenicity endpoint: Anti-HBs positivity >=10 mIU/mL, GMT, and maintenance of anti-HBs >=100 mIU/mL
Clinical endpoint: Anti-HBc seroconversion and HBsAg positivity over 8 years
Safety endpoint: Not reported
NI result: Reduced dose gave lower early antibody responses but similar long-term protection
Key numerical result(s): 574 babies were assigned to 5 microg or 2.5 microg regimens. The regular dose had higher anti-HBs positivity, GMTs, and maintenance of anti-HBs >=100 mIU/mL through year 1. Over 8 years, only 1% had anti-HBc seroconversion and none became HBsAg positive.
Data extraction notes: Useful because it separates early antibody differences from long-term clinical protection in a neonatal program context.
Screening note: Primary reduced-dose hepatitis B schedule study
Abstract: Five hundred and seventy-four babies born to HBsAg negative mothers in Hong Kong received either a regular (5 micro g) or reduced (2.5 micro g) three-dose regimen of recombinant hepatitis B vaccine. A significantly higher anti-HBs positivity rate (>or=10 mIU/ml), geometric mean titer (GMT) and the maintenance of a high anti-HBs level (>or=100 mIU/ml) were observed with the regular-dose regimen. The differences persisted, however, only up to 1 year post-vaccination. Over an 8-year period, only 1% of the vaccinees demonstrated anti-HBc seroconversion and none had become HBsAg positive. The long-term efficacy of the reduced-dose regimen was confirmed, even in an HBV endemic population.
TL;DR / study gist: The reduced-dose newborn hepatitis B schedule produced weaker antibody levels early on, but over eight years it still appeared to protect well, with very little evidence of breakthrough infection and no HBsAg positivity. This makes it an important reminder that lower short-term titers do not always mean lower long-term effectiveness.
Original abstract: Five hundred and seventy-four babies born to HBsAg negative mothers in Hong Kong received either a regular (5 micro g) or reduced (2.5 micro g) three-dose regimen of recombinant hepatitis B vaccine. Title: Randomized trial of the immunogenicity of fractional dose regimens of PRP-T Haemophilus influenzae type b conjugate vaccine
Authors: Fernández, J.; Balter, S.; Feris, J.; Gómez, E.; Garib, Z.; Castellanos, P. L.; Sánchez, J.; Romero-Steiner, S.; Levine, O. S.
Journal / source: The American Journal of Tropical Medicine and Hygiene
Vaccine / intervention: PRP-T Haemophilus influenzae type b conjugate vaccine
Route of administration: Not clearly stated in abstract; separate injection or combined with DTP
Comparator (full dose / standard / other: Full-dose PRP-T given separately or combined with DTP
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): Half-dose and one-third-dose PRP-T given separately or combined with DTP
Schedule / timing: At ages 2, 4, and 6 months; assessed 1 month post-dose 3
Primary endpoint: Whether fractional PRP-T regimens achieve acceptable antibody responses
Immunogenicity endpoint: Anti-PRP proportion >1.0 microg/mL in >70% and >0.15 microg/mL in >90%; GMCs 1 month after dose 3
Safety endpoint: Not reported in abstract
Non-inferiority margin: Protocol-defined acceptable response criteria, not formal NI margin
NI result: All regimens met predefined acceptable response criteria
Key numerical result(s): 600 children were assigned to six regimens. One month after dose 3, all regimens met acceptability criteria. When Hib was given separately, GMCs were 11.2, 11.9, and 16.3 microg/mL in full-, half-, and one-third-dose groups; when combined with DTP, GMCs were 6.4, 5.2, and 5.7 microg/mL.
Data extraction notes: Strong fractional-dose Hib trial with clear predefined antibody thresholds; full text needed for complete safety and allocation details.
Screening note: Primary fractional-dose Hib conjugate vaccine trial
Abstract: To assess the immunogenicity of more economical regimens of Haemophilus influenzae type b (Hib) conjugate vaccine, a randomized trial of fractional doses of polyribosylribitol phosphate-tetanus toxoid (PRP-T) Hib vaccine was undertaken in the Dominican Republic. Six hundred children were assigned to one of six regimens with PRP-T vaccine: full-dose, half-dose, and one-third-dose of Hib vaccine given separately or combined with diphtheria, tetanus, and pertussis (DTP) vaccine at ages 2, 4, and 6 months. Regimens that elicited antibody levels > 1.0 microg/mL in >70% of children and < or = 0.15 microg/mL in > 90% of children were considered acceptable. At 1 month post Dose 3, all regimens met the criteria for acceptable response. Among those who received Hib as a separate injection, geometric mean concentrations of anti-PRP bodies (GMCs) at age 1 month post Dose 3 were 11.2, 11.9, and 16.3 in the full, half, and one-third dose groups, respectively. Among those who received Hib and DTP combined, the GMCs were 6.4, 5.2, and 5.7 in the full-, half-, and one-third-dose groups respectively.
TL;DR / study gist: This Dominican Republic Hib trial found that even half-dose and one-third-dose PRP-T schedules still met prespecified antibody criteria after the primary series. The data suggest meaningful scope for dose-sparing, especially when the vaccine was given separately rather than mixed with DTP.
