Title: Reactogenicity and immunogenicity of reduced antigen content diphtheria-tetanus-acellular pertussis vaccines as a booster in 4-7-year-old children primed with diphtheria-tetanus-whole cell pertussis vaccine before 2 years of age
Authors: Dagan, R.; Igbaria, K.; Piglansky, L.; Van Brusteghem, F.; Melot, V.; Kaufhold, A.
Special population: Children primed with whole-cell pertussis vaccine before age 2 years
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): Reduced-antigen dtpa booster; experimental low-dose dtpa formulation
Schedule / timing: Single booster at age 4-7 years; assessed 1 month after vaccination
Primary endpoint: Reactogenicity and post-booster antibody response compared across full-dose and reduced-antigen formulations
Immunogenicity endpoint: Postvaccination antibody concentrations to diphtheria, tetanus, and pertussis antigens
Safety endpoint: Solicited local, systemic, and unsolicited symptoms for 15 days; serious adverse events
NI result: Reduced-antigen licensed vaccine produced antibody concentrations comparable to full dose; experimental lower-dose formulation was less immunogenic
Key numerical result(s): 225/226 children completed the study. Symptoms were reported by 60.0%-66.7% across groups, mostly mild/moderate. After vaccination, 98.7%-100% had antibodies to the three pertussis antigens, and all had seroprotective diphtheria/tetanus concentrations. The reduced-antigen licensed vaccine was comparable to full dose, while the experimental low-dose formulation had significantly lower diphtheria, tetanus, and anti-FHA responses.
Data extraction notes: Useful pediatric reduced-antigen booster study; full text would be needed for exact group sizes and numerical GMTs by arm.
Screening note: Primary reduced-antigen pertussis booster study
TL;DR / study gist: In children previously primed with whole-cell pertussis vaccine, a reduced-antigen licensed dTpa booster looked as immunogenic and as well tolerated as the full-dose booster. An even lower experimental formulation was less immunogenic, suggesting some antigen reduction is acceptable but there is a lower limit.
Original abstract: BACKGROUND: The recent introduction of acellular pertussis vaccines (Pa) offers the possibility of booster doses in older children and adults. This can be conveniently accomplished by combining acellular pertussis antigens with diphtheria and tetanus toxoids. However the optimal dosage for the booster injection has not yet been determined. OBJECTIVE: To compare the reactogenicity and immunogenicity of diphtheria-tetanus-acellular pertussis vaccines (DTPa) with lower antigen contents to a licensed DTPa vaccine when given at 4-7 years of age as a booster to DTPw-primed children. METHODS: Two hundred and twenty-six children primed with four doses of DTPw before 2 years of age were enrolled and allocated to three groups to receive one dose of either DTPa (Infanrix, SmithKline Beecham, Biologicals), a reduced antigen formulation of this vaccine (dtpa, SmithKline Beecham Biologicals), or an experimental low dose formulation (dtpa-exp; d and t, Michigan Biologic Products Institute). Reactogenicity was assessed using diary cards for 15 days. Immunogenicity was determined as antibody responses against the vaccine components in pre- and 1 month postvaccination sera. RESULTS: Of the 225 children who completed the study, 60.0-66.7% reported symptoms, with no significant differences in rates between groups. Local, systemic and unsolicited symptoms occurred with similar frequencies in all three groups, the vast majority (> 90%) being considered as mild or moderate. No serious adverse events related to vaccination were reported. After vaccination, all subjects displayed seroprotective concentrations against diphtheria and tetanus, and 98.7-100% had antibodies against the three pertussis component antigens. The group receiving the reduced dose of the licensed vaccine showed antibody concentrations comparable to those of the full dose group. However, the group receiving the experimental low dose formulation had statistically significantly lower antibody concentrations against both diphtheria and tetanus toxoids compared with the two other groups, as well as significantly lower anti FHA antibody concentrations. CONCLUSIONS: Reducing the antigen content of dtpa had no deleterious effect on the immunogenicity of the vaccine when given as a fifth dose at 4-7 years of age in DTPw-primed children. The reactogenicity profile of both the reduced antigen dtpa vaccines and DTPa were acceptable, the vast majority of local and systemic reactions being considered as mild to moderate, with no vaccine-related serious adverse events reported. The use of lower antigen content dtpa vaccine as a booster in children aged 4-7 is safe and immunogenic.

Title: Economisation of vaccination against Haemophilus influenzae type b: a randomised trial of immunogenicity of fractional-dose and two-dose regimens
Authors: Lagos, R.; Valenzuela, M. T.; Levine, O. S.; Losonsky, G. A.; Erazo, A.; Wasserman, S. S.; Levine, M. M.
Vaccine / intervention: Hib conjugate vaccines PRP-T and PRP-CRM197 under full-dose, fractional-dose, and two-dose regimens
Comparator (full dose / standard / other: Three full doses of PRP-T or PRP-CRM197
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): Three half-dose regimens; three one-third-dose regimens; two-dose full-dose regimen at 4 and 6 months
Schedule / timing: Vaccination aligned with DTP at 2, 4, and 6 months; primary assessment at age 8 months
Primary endpoint: Proportion with anti-PRP concentration >=0.15 microg/mL at age 8 months
Immunogenicity endpoint: Serum anti-PRP concentrations >=0.15 microg/mL
Safety endpoint: Not reported in abstract
NI result: Fractional-dose regimens performed similarly to full-dose schedules on the main antibody threshold; two-dose PRP-T also performed well, while two-dose PRP-CRM197 was somewhat weaker
Key numerical result(s): 627 Chilean infants were allocated to eight groups. At age 8 months, 93% of infants receiving three full doses of PRP-T and 95% receiving three full doses of PRP-CRM197 reached anti-PRP >=0.15 microg/mL. Corresponding rates for fractional-dose regimens ranged from 91% to 100%. Two-dose schedules gave 99% for PRP-T and 87% for PRP-CRM197.
Data extraction notes: Strong infant Hib economization study with clear seroprotection threshold; full text needed for exact arm-by-arm doses and route details.
Screening note: Primary fractional/two-dose Hib trial
TL;DR / study gist: This Chilean Hib trial found that half-dose and one-third-dose regimens could generate antibody responses very similar to a standard three-dose schedule. It is one of the clearest early demonstrations that conjugate-vaccine dose-sparing can work in infants.
Original abstract: BACKGROUND: The cost of Haemophilus influenzae type b (Hib) conjugate vaccines has limited their use in non-industrialised countries. To identify more economical vaccination schedules, we carried out a randomised trial of the immunogenicity of alternative regimens to the standard three-dose series. METHODS: 627 Chilean infants were randomly allocated to one of four regimens with either Hib polysaccharide-tetanus toxoid conjugate vaccine (PRP-T) or Hib oligosaccharide-diphtheria mutant toxoid conjugate vaccine (PRP-CRM197), for a total of eight groups. All infants receive diphtheria-tetanus-pertussis (DTP) vaccine at ages 2, 4, and 6 months. The regimens included three full doses, three fractional doses consisting of one half or one third of the full dose, and a regimen of two full doses (at age 4 and 6 months). The primary outcome was the proportion of infants with serum anti-polyribosylribitol phosphate (PRP, the type b capsular polysaccharide) concentrations of 0.15 microg/mL or more at age 8 months. FINDINGS: 93% (95% CI 85-98) of infants vaccinated with three full doses of PRP-T or PRP-CRM197 (95% CI 84-98) achieved anti-PRP concentrations of 0.15 microg/mL or more at age 8 months, compared with 91% (83-96) to 100% (95-100) of infants immunised with any fractional-dose regimen. Of the infants vaccinated with two doses of PRP-T or PRP-CRM197, 99% (93-100) and 87% (77-93) developed anti-PRP concentrations of 0.15 microg/mL or more, respectively. INTERPRETATION: 91% (83-96) to 100% (95-100) of infants immunised with one-half or one-third of a full dose of Hib conjugate developed protective antibody concentrations. Carrier priming with DTP may make two-dose schedules an option in some places. These alternative regimens could bring the cost of Hib vaccines within reach of countries that currently cannot afford them.

Title: Hepatitis B vaccination: half dose recombinant DNA hepatitis B vaccine (B-Hepavac II) is as immunogenic as the full recommended dose in healthy adults
Authors: Guan, R.; Yap, I.; Tay, H. H.
Vaccine / intervention: Recombinant DNA hepatitis B vaccine (B-Hepavac II)
Special population: Healthy adults with no previous HBV exposure
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): 5 microg half-dose regimen
Schedule / timing: 0, 1, and 6 months; antibody assessed 2 months after third dose
Primary endpoint: Anti-HBs seroconversion and anti-HBs >10 IU/L after completion of the series
Immunogenicity endpoint: Seroconversion; anti-HBs >10 IU/L; GMT
Safety endpoint: Fever and mild injection-site pain
NI result: Half dose was reported to be as immunogenic as the standard dose
Key numerical result(s): 67 adults aged 20-40 years were randomized. Two months after the third dose, 100% had seroconverted; 97% of the 10 microg group and 91% of the 5 microg group had anti-HBs >10 IU/L. GMTs were 891 IU/L and 923 IU/L, respectively, with no significant difference.
Data extraction notes: Clean adult hepatitis B half-dose comparison; useful for binary seroprotection meta-analysis, though arm denominators would ideally be confirmed from full text.
Screening note: Primary half-dose hepatitis B equivalence study
TL;DR / study gist: In healthy Singaporean adults, three intramuscular doses of 5 microg recombinant hepatitis B vaccine performed much like the standard 10 microg schedule. Seroconversion was universal, GMTs were similar, and the authors concluded the half dose was effectively equivalent.
Original abstract: Sixty-seven healthy adult volunteers aged 20-40 years with no previous exposure to hepatitis B virus were randomized to receive either a 10 micrograms or 5 micrograms dose of recombinant DNA hepatitis B (HB) vaccine (B-Hepavac II) intramuscularly at 0, 1 and 6 months. Two months after the third injection 100% of subjects had seroconverted: 97% of the 10 micrograms group and 91% of the 5 micrograms group had antibody to HB surface antigen (anti-HBs) levels greater than 10 iu/L. The geometric mean titres (GMT) of anti-HBs levels at this time were 891 iu/L in the 10 micrograms dose group and 923 iu/L in the 5 micrograms dose group. These differences were not significant. Adverse effects included fever and mild pain at the injection site. The reduced dose of 5 micrograms was as effective as the standard 10 micrograms dose.

Title: A comparative immunogenicity-reactogenicity dose-response study of influenza vaccine
Authors: Guarnaccia, S.; Peters, S. M.; Habib, F.; Mancuso, G. R.; Dibenedetto, S. P.; Espey, M.; Bellanti, J. A.
Vaccine / intervention: Inactivated influenza vaccine at varying reduced volumes
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): 0.1 mL and 0.05 mL reduced doses
Schedule / timing: Single vaccination; adverse events over 7 days and HAI responses over 28 days
Primary endpoint: Dose-response of immunogenicity and reactogenicity
Immunogenicity endpoint: HAI antibody responses to A/Leningrad, A/Taiwan, and B/Ann Arbor antigens
Safety endpoint: Incidence of adverse reactions over 7 days
NI result: Reduced doses produced comparable patterns of antibody rise but with lower magnitude; not a formal non-inferiority study
Key numerical result(s): 29 healthy adults received 0.5 mL, 0.1 mL, or 0.05 mL vaccine. Adverse reactions were significantly less frequent in the 0.1 mL and 0.05 mL groups than in the 0.5 mL group. HAI responses to three influenza antigens rose in all groups, but the magnitude of response was lower at lower doses.
Reason not meta-analyzable: Very small early dose-response study with limited arm sizes and no formal binary seroprotection outcome in the abstract.
Data extraction notes: Useful historical proof-of-concept for influenza dose reduction, but likely less suitable for pooled quantitative synthesis without full text.
Screening note: Primary human dose-response influenza vaccine study with reduced-dose arms.
TL;DR / study gist: This small influenza dose-response study suggested that reducing the vaccine volume lowered reactogenicity and still generated antibody rises against all tested strains, though the immune response weakened as the dose got smaller. It supports the idea of dose-sparing, but not a clear equivalence claim.
Original abstract: This study examined the immunogenic and reactogenic responses of influenza vaccine in 29 healthy nonallergic adults at three vaccine dosages: 0.5 mL, 0.1 mL, and 0.05 mL. After immunization a 7-day assessment of adverse reactions was made and serial serum hemagglutination-inhibition (HAI) antibody responses were measured during a 28-day period. The incidence of adverse reactions was significantly decreased in the group receiving 0.1 mL and 0.05 mL compared with the group receiving 0.5 mL of vaccine. After immunization with 0.1 mL or 0.05 mL vaccine increases in serum HAI antibody to A/Leningrad, A/Taiwan, and B/Ann Arbor influenza antigens were seen comparable to those observed after 0.5 mL. However the magnitude of these rises were lower and were directly correlated with the dose of vaccine. Since immunization of egg-sensitive allergic patients with influenza vaccine poses a risk of localized and systemic reactions, a common clinical practice is to prevent such reactions by vaccine dilution. Although the results of the present study suggest that vaccine dilution results in a decrease in adverse reactions, there is also the risk of decrease protective immunity with this procedure and therefore the practice should not be condoned.

Title: Immunogenicity of a low dose recombinant DNA hepatitis B vaccine in healthy adults in Singapore
Authors: Guan, R.; Tay, H. H.; Yap, I.; Smith, R.; Tan, L. H.
Journal / source: Asian Pacific Journal of Allergy and Immunology
Vaccine / intervention: Yeast-derived recombinant DNA hepatitis B vaccine (B-Hepavac II)
Special population: Healthy adults with no previous hepatitis B exposure
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): 5 microg half-dose regimen
Schedule / timing: 0, 1, and 6 months, with additional follow-up to 12 months and booster at month 14 for low responders
Sample size analyzed: 84 recruited; 43 received 5 microg and 41 received 10 microg
Primary endpoint: Anti-HBs seroconversion and anti-HBs >10 IU/L over time
Immunogenicity endpoint: Anti-HBs >=2.1 IU/L, anti-HBs >10 IU/L, and GMT over 12 months
Safety endpoint: Diary-card local/systemic adverse effects; ALT, HBsAg, anti-HBc monitoring
NI result: Half dose appeared as immunogenic as the standard dose
Key numerical result(s): 84 adults were randomized: 43 to 5 microg and 41 to 10 microg. One month after the second dose, seroconversion rates were 85% and 86%, respectively. Two months after the third dose, 100% seroconverted; anti-HBs >10 IU/L was seen in 95% of the 10 microg group and 91% of the 5 microg group. GMTs at months 2, 6, 8, and 12 in the 10 microg group were 34, 61, 811, and 188 IU/L versus 26, 45, 836, and 304 IU/L in the 5 microg group.
Data extraction notes: This open PDF gives more numerical detail than many early hepatitis B studies. It may represent an earlier or overlapping cohort related to S083, so duplication should be checked before synthesis.
Screening note: Primary low-dose hepatitis B vaccine study
TL;DR / study gist: This Singapore trial found that 5 microg recombinant hepatitis B vaccine generated very similar seroconversion and GMTs to the standard 10 microg schedule in healthy adults. It is one of the cleaner early examples of successful half-dose hepatitis B vaccination.
Original abstract: The immunogenicity and safety of a standard dose of 10 micrograms of a yeast derived recombinant DNA hepatitis B vaccine (B-Hepavac II) was compared with that of a reduced dose of 5 micrograms in 84 healthy adult volunteers with no previous exposure to hepatitis B. Each subject received either a 10 micrograms or 5 micrograms dose of vaccine intramuscularly at 0, 1 and 6 months. One month after the second injection of vaccine the seroconversion rate in the two groups were 85 and 86 percent respectively. Two months after the third injection 100 percent of participants had sero-converted; 95 percent of the 10 micrograms group and 91 percent of the 5 micrograms group had titres of anti-HBs greater than 10 IU/L. The geometric mean titres (GMT) of anti-HBs levels at 2, 6, 8, and 12 months were 34, 61, 811 and 188 IU/L in the 10 micrograms group and 26, 45, 836 and 304 IU/L in the 5 micrograms group respectively. Adverse effects were mild and transient. The vaccine was safe and immunogenic in the doses given. The reduced dose of 5 micrograms was as effective as the standard 10 micrograms dose.

Title: Hepatitis B immunization in high risk neonates born from HBsAg and HBeAg positive mothers: comparison of standard and low dose regimens
Authors: Pongpipat, D.; Suvatte, V.; Assateerawatts, A.
Journal / source: Asian Pacific Journal of Allergy and Immunology
Vaccine / intervention: Plasma-derived hepatitis B vaccine (Hevac B) with HBIG prophylaxis
Special population: High-risk neonates born to HBsAg- and HBeAg-positive carrier mothers
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): 2 microg HBV vaccine plus HBIG
Schedule / timing: HBIG immediately after birth; vaccine at birth, 1, 2, and 12 months
Primary endpoint: Protective efficacy against perinatal HBV transmission and antibody response
Immunogenicity endpoint: Antibody responses after combined prophylaxis
Safety endpoint: Not reported in abstract
NI result: Reduced dose had similar efficacy and antibody response to standard dose
Key numerical result(s): 40 high-risk neonates were split into two groups of 20. Group I received 5 microg vaccine and group II received 2 microg vaccine, both with HBIG 100 IU after birth. Protective efficacy was 94.0% in the standard-dose group and 93.2% in the reduced-dose group, with no statistically significant difference in efficacy or antibody responses.
Data extraction notes: Important neonatal prevention study combining HBIG with reduced-dose vaccine; full text would be needed for exact transmission/serology definitions.
Screening note: Primary comparison of standard vs low-dose hepatitis B regimens
TL;DR / study gist: Among neonates at very high risk of perinatal hepatitis B transmission, a 2 microg vaccine schedule combined with HBIG looked nearly identical to a 5 microg schedule. This suggests meaningful dose-sparing may be possible even in post-exposure newborn prophylaxis.
Original abstract: A reduced dose of plasma derived hepatitis B vaccine (Hevac B) was tested for efficacy in the prevention of perinatal hepatitis B virus (HBV) transmission in high risk neonates born from e-antigen positive HBsAg carrier mothers. Forty newborn infants born of these mothers were given hepatitis B immune globulin (HBIG) 100 IU intramuscularly immediately after birth, combined with either standard or reduced doses of HBV vaccine. The infants were divided into two groups of 20 infants each. The standard dose of HBV vaccine (5 micrograms) was given to group I, while infants in group II received reduced dose (2 micrograms) at birth and at 1, 2 and 12 months of age. There was no statistically significant difference in the efficacy and antibody responses of these two combined prophylaxis regimens. The protective efficacy rate of HBV vaccine was found to be 94.0 and 93.2 percent in group I and group II, respectively. At twelve months of age, the anti-HBs seroconversion rates were 80.0 percent in group I and 86.7 percent in group II, with geometric mean titres of 84.57 mlU/ml and 78.56 mlU/ml, in group I and group II, respectively. One month after a booster at one year of age, anti-HBs could be detected in 86.7 percent of the infants in both groups. The geometric mean titres were 429.04 and 664.81 mlU/ml, in group I and group II, respectively. Anti-PreS2 antibody was detected in high titre as early as 4 months after the first dose of HBV vaccine, with a geometric mean titre of 116.30 mlU/ml and 107.97 mlU/ml, in group I and group II, respectively.(ABSTRACT TRUNCATED AT 250 WORDS)

Title: Immunogenicity of a reduced dose of recombinant hepatitis B vaccine
Authors: Roumeliotou-Karayannis, A.; Dandolos, E.; Richardson, S. C.; Papaevangelou, G.
Comparator (full dose / standard / other: Historic/previous 5 microg and 10 microg trial results rather than concurrent control in the abstract
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): 2.5 microg reduced-dose regimen
Schedule / timing: Over seven months
Primary endpoint: Anti-HBs response to 2.5 microg recombinant vaccine
Immunogenicity endpoint: Seroconversion; anti-HBs >=10 IU/L; anti-HBs >=100 IU/L; GMT
Safety endpoint: Not reported in abstract
NI result: Reduced 2.5 microg dose was inferior and probably too low for general adult use
Key numerical result(s): 54 males aged 17-19 years received 2.5 microg recombinant hepatitis B vaccine. All seroconverted, but 3 participants (6%) remained below the protective threshold of 10 IU/L and 8 (15%) remained below 100 IU/L. Final GMT was 810 IU/L (95% CI 433 to 1520).
Reason not meta-analyzable: Comparator appears to be from equivalent historical trials rather than a clearly concurrent randomized control group in the abstract.
Data extraction notes: Useful lower-bound dose-finding study showing 2.5 microg may be too low in young men; clarify design from full text before pooling.
Screening note: Primary reduced-dose recombinant hepatitis B vaccine study
TL;DR / study gist: This study pushed the hepatitis B dose low enough to show where performance begins to drop. Although everyone seroconverted after 2.5 microg, a meaningful minority failed to achieve clearly protective or robust antibody levels, so the authors judged this dose too low for routine use.
Original abstract: We report a trial of a reduced dose (2.5 micrograms) of recombinant hepatitis B vaccine, carried out over seven months in 54 males aged 17 to 19 years. Response to the reduced dose was significantly inferior to that found in equivalent trials of 5 and 10 micrograms doses. Although all seroconverted, three participants (6%) remained below the protective level of 10 IU l-1 of antibody to hepatitis B surface antigen (anti-HBs) and a further eight (15%) below 100 IU l-1, possibly requiring early revaccination. The final geometric mean titre of anti-HBs was 810 IU l-1 (95% confidence interval, 433 to 1520). It appears that 2.5 micrograms may be too low a dosage for general use in adults.

Title: Hepatitis B virus vaccine: a randomized trial of a reduced dose regimen in hemodialysis patients
Authors: Aronoff, G. R.; Maxwell, D. R.; Batteiger, B. E.; Fineberg, N. S.
Journal / source: American Journal of Kidney Diseases: The Official Journal of the National Kidney Foundation
Vaccine / intervention: Hepatitis B virus vaccine in hemodialysis patients
Comparator (full dose / standard / other: Three 40 mcg intramuscular injections over 6 months
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): Three 20 mcg intramuscular injections over 6 months
Schedule / timing: Three injections over 6 months
Sample size randomized: 24 hemodialysis patients; 19 normal volunteers also received 20 mcg
Primary endpoint: Anti-HBs response in dialysis patients comparing half and full recommended dose
Immunogenicity endpoint: Qualitative and quantitative Anti-HBs response
Safety endpoint: Not reported in abstract
NI result: Half dose was not inferior in dialysis patients; doubling the dose did not improve response
Key numerical result(s): 24 hemodialysis patients were randomized to three 20 mcg or three 40 mcg doses over 6 months. Non-uremic volunteers seroconverted more often than dialysis patients. Within dialysis patients, doubling the dose did not improve response, leading the authors to conclude that the half dose did not diminish vaccine response in this group.
Data extraction notes: Important special-population hepatitis B trial showing no benefit from dose escalation in dialysis; full text needed for exact responder counts.
Screening note: Primary reduced-dose hepatitis B trial in hemodialysis
TL;DR / study gist: In hemodialysis patients, increasing hepatitis B vaccine dose from 20 to 40 microg did not improve immune response. That makes this an early pragmatic cost-saving study suggesting that the smaller dose may be enough even in a high-risk population with generally weaker vaccine responses.
Original abstract: To test the hypothesis that increasing the dose enhances response to hepatitis B virus vaccine in hemodialysis patients, we performed a randomized, double blind, controlled clinical trial. Twenty-four hemodialysis patients were randomly assigned to receive either three 20 mcg or the recommended three 40 mcg intramuscular injections over 6 months. In addition, 19 normal volunteers also received three 20 mcg doses of the vaccine. The presence of Anti-HBs was determined qualitatively and quantitatively. Non-uremic subjects seroconverted more frequently than did either of the dialysis patient groups. Doubling the individual doses of vaccine did not improve the response of the dialysis patients. We conclude that the response to the vaccine is not diminished when dialysis patients are given half the recommended dose of the vaccine and that the cost of vaccinating this high-risk population could be substantially reduced.

Title: Vaccination against hepatitis B in Nigerian children: trials of reduced dose and intradermal vaccine
Authors: Ayoola, E. A.
Vaccine / intervention: Hevac B hepatitis B vaccine at reduced dose by subcutaneous or intradermal route
Comparator (full dose / standard / other: Subcutaneous reduced-dose regimen in first trial component
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): 2 microg subcutaneous and 2 microg intradermal regimens
Schedule / timing: Three monthly doses
Primary endpoint: Anti-HBs positivity after reduced-dose subcutaneous versus intradermal vaccination
Immunogenicity endpoint: Anti-HBs positivity one month after completing the schedule
Safety endpoint: Adverse side-effects
NI result: Reduced-dose subcutaneous and intradermal regimens both induced responses, with somewhat lower response by intradermal route
Key numerical result(s): 197 Nigerian children received three monthly doses of Hevac B: 96 received 2 microg subcutaneously and 101 received 2 microg intradermally. One month after the schedule, 82.3% of the subcutaneous group and 74.3% of the intradermal group were anti-HBs positive, without adverse side-effects.
Reason not meta-analyzable: No standard full-dose comparator is described in the available abstract/snippet; this may be more useful as a descriptive reduced-dose study.
Data extraction notes: PubMed entry has no abstract; key details come from a cited summary/snippet. Full paper would be needed before high-confidence quantitative pooling.
Screening note: Primary reduced-dose/intradermal hepatitis B vaccine trial
TL;DR / study gist: This Nigerian pediatric hepatitis B study suggests that very small doses can still produce useful seroconversion, especially when given subcutaneously rather than intradermally. It is relevant as an early low-resource dose-economization study, but the lack of a clearly described full-dose comparator makes it harder to synthesize quantitatively.

Title: Trial of a reduced dose of measles vaccine in Nigerian children
Authors: Wallace, R. B.; Landrigan, P. J.; Smith, E. A.; Pifer, J.; Teller, B.; Foster, S. O.
Journal / source: Bulletin of the World Health Organization
Vaccine / intervention: Schwarz live attenuated measles vaccine at reduced doses
Special population: Seronegative children aged 7-30 months in rural villages
Comparator (full dose / standard / other: Full standard measles vaccine dose in 0.5 mL
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): 40%, 60%, and 80% reductions in vaccine virus dosage; reduced inoculum volume below 0.5 mL
Schedule / timing: Single vaccination; seroconversion assessment after vaccination
Primary endpoint: Seroconversion after reduced-dose measles vaccination
Immunogenicity endpoint: Seroconversion rate
Safety endpoint: Not reported in abstract
NI result: Reduced doses were clearly inferior; dose reduction was not a satisfactory economization strategy
Key numerical result(s): 967 seronegative children aged 7-30 months were vaccinated. In children older than 12 months, seroconversion fell progressively from 89% with full dose to as low as 46% with reduced doses. Lowering inoculum volume below 0.5 mL further reduced seroconversion, and responses were lower in children younger than 12 months across all groups.
Data extraction notes: Large early field trial showing clear loss of immunogenicity with measles dose reduction; useful as a negative dose-sparing study.
Screening note: Randomized pediatric influenza study including reduced-dose intradermal vaccination arms.
TL;DR / study gist: This large Nigerian measles trial found that cutting the vaccine dose sharply reduced seroconversion, especially in younger children. It is a useful counterexample in the literature because it shows that dose-sparing does not work equally well for every vaccine.
Original abstract: To evaluate the effectiveness of measles vaccine in reduced dosage, Schwarz live, further attenuated measles virus vaccine was given in various doses by jet injector to 967 seronegative children aged 7-30 months in rural villages in the Northwest State of Nigeria. For children over 12 months of age, reducing vaccine virus dosage by 40%, 60%, or 80% led to progressive and statistically significant reductions in the seroconversion rates from 89% in children receiving the full dose to a minimum value of 46%. Reducing the volume of the inoculum below the standard of 0.5 ml resulted in a further reduction of seroconversion rates at each antigen dose level. Seroconversion rates in all dosage groups were lower for children below 12 months of age. These data suggest that dose reduction would not be a satisfactory economy measure in measles vaccination campaigns.

Title: Immunogenicity and tolerability of a paediatric presentation of a virosomal hepatitis A vaccine in Chilean children aged 1-16 years
Authors: Katia Abarca, Isabel Ibáñez, Pablo de la Fuente, Leonardo Cerda, Jorge Bergeret, Gert Frösner, Humberto Ibarra
Vaccine / intervention: Epaxal (virosomal hepatitis A vaccine) 0.25 mL paediatric dose
Population age group: Children and adolescents 12 months to <17 years
Special population: Healthy children not previously vaccinated against hepatitis A
Study design: Open, randomised, controlled, multi-centre, parallel-group study
Comparator (full dose / standard / other: Epaxal 0.5 mL (standard adult dose) and Havrix Junior 0.5 mL
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): Epaxal 0.25 mL
Schedule / timing: Day 1 and Month 6 (26 weeks ± 14 days)
Sample size analyzed: 341 in ATP population for primary immunogenicity at Month 1
Primary endpoint: Proportion of subjects seroprotected (anti-HAV antibody concentration >= 10 mIU/mL) in the ATP population at Month 1
Immunogenicity endpoint: Seroprotection rate (>= 10 mIU/mL and >= 20 mIU/mL) at Month 1 and Month 7, and anti-HAV geometric mean concentrations (GMC)
Safety endpoint: Frequency of solicited local and systemic adverse events (AEs) and unsolicited AEs
Non-inferiority margin: Lower limit of the two-sided 95% confidence interval (CI) strictly greater than -10% for difference in seroprotection rate
NI result: Met (Epaxal 0.25 mL was non-inferior to Epaxal 0.5 mL at Month 1, and to Havrix Junior at Months 1 and 6)
Key numerical result(s): Seroprotection rates at Month 1: 95.7% (Epaxal 0.25 mL), 99.3% (Epaxal 0.5 mL), and 94.0% (Havrix Junior). At Month 7: 100% across all groups.
Reason not meta-analyzable: N/A
Data extraction notes: Month 6 immunogenicity data was largely missing due to a laboratory fire, but substitute post-booster Month 7 samples were successfully obtained.
Screening note: N/A
Abstract: The study assessed the non-inferiority of a 0.25 mL paediatric dose of Epaxal compared to the 0.5 mL standard dose and Havrix Junior in 360 Chilean children. The paediatric dose demonstrated high seroprotection rates (95.7% at Month 1, 100% post-booster), fulfilling the non-inferiority criteria, and was well tolerated across all age groups.
TL;DR / study gist: A 0.25 mL paediatric dose of the virosomal hepatitis A vaccine Epaxal is highly immunogenic and well-tolerated, demonstrating non-inferiority to the standard 0.5 mL dose and Havrix Junior in children aged 1-16.
Original abstract: We assessed the immunogenicity of the paediatric dose of Epaxal (0.25 mL) and the degrees of seroprotection achieved with the standard dose (0.5 mL) of Epaxal® or a dose of Havrix® Junior, in children in an open, randomised, controlled, multi-centre, parallel-group study conducted at 2 Chilean study centres. 360 healthy children and adolescents 12 months to <17 years of age not previously vaccinated against hepatitis A were enrolled. Subjects were randomised 2:2:1 to be vaccinated with either Epaxal 0.25 mL [n=146], Epaxal® 0.5 mL [n=142] or Havrix® Junior [n=72] intramuscularly on Day 1 and after 6 months (26 weeks ± 14 days). Primary end point was the proportion of subjects seroprotected (anti-HAV antibody concentration ≥ 10 mIU/mL) in the ATP population at Month 1. All vaccines elicited high seroprotection rates at Month 1: 95.7% with Epaxal® 0.25 mL, 99.3% with Epaxal® 0.5 mL and 94.0% with Havrix® Junior. After the booster vaccination, all subjects demonstrated 100% seroprotection with all vaccines. Antibody concentrations were similarly high in all age groups. The paediatric presentation achieved antibody concentrations similar to those achieved with the 0.5 mL dose across the entire age range, and there were no differences across the range of body weights from 9.0 kg to 82.7 kg. All study vaccines were well tolerated and there were no AEs leading to discontinuation. Thus, the paediatric 0.25 mL dose of Epaxal fulfilled the primary objective of showing non-inferiority to the adult 0.5 mL dose and to Havrix® Junior, in terms of seroprotection rates achieved. The results show the paediatric dose of Epaxal to be an attractive option when conducting childhood-vaccination programmes.

Title: Immunogenicity of a recombinant, yeast-derived, anti-hepatitis-B vaccine after alternative dosage and schedule vaccination in Pakistani children
Authors: Dure Samin Akram, Sajid Maqbool, Dure Shawar Khan, Rafat Jafri, Sajid Randhawa, Carmen Valenzuela-Silva, Pedro Lopez-Saura
Vaccine / intervention: Heberbiovac HB (recombinant, P. pastoris-derived hepatitis B vaccine)
Special population: Healthy children negative for HBsAg and anti-HBsAg
Comparator (full dose / standard / other: Group B: 10 µg at 0, 1, and 2 months (standard 3-dose schedule)
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): Group A: 10 µg at 0 and 1 months (2-dose schedule); Group C: 5 µg at 0, 1, and 2 months (fractional 3-dose schedule)
Schedule / timing: Group A: 0, 1 months; Groups B & C: 0, 1, 2 months. Evaluated 2 months after last dose (and 1-year follow-up).
Sample size analyzed: 990 evaluated (Group A: 327; Group B: 326; Group C: 337)
Primary endpoint: Seroprotection rate (titers > 10 IU/L) and hyperresponse rate (titers > 100 IU/L) 2 months after last dose
Immunogenicity endpoint: Anti-HBsAg geometric mean titers (GMT), seroprotection rates, and hyperresponse rates
Safety endpoint: Clinical evaluation for adverse events
NI result: N/A
Key numerical result(s): Seroprotection rates 2 months post-last dose: Group A (10µg x2) = 100%; Group B (10µg x3) = 99.7%; Group C (5µg x3) = 99.7%. Hyperresponse rates: Group A = 92.7%; Group B = 99.4%; Group C = 97.0%. GMT (IU/L): Group A = 500; Group B = 836; Group C = 654.
Reason not meta-analyzable: N/A
Data extraction notes: Reduced dose (5µg) and reduced schedule (2 doses of 10µg) achieved very high seroprotection rates (>99%) comparable to the standard schedule, though overall hyperresponse and GMT were slightly lower.
Screening note: Good quality RCT assessing both a reduced schedule (2 doses) and a fractional dose (5µg instead of 10µg) of Hep B vaccine.
Abstract: A controlled, randomized trial was conducted in urban areas of Karachi and Lahore with the aim to look for ways to improve the cost-effectiveness of hepatitis B vaccination. Children under 15 years old (including neonates) were selected and screened for immunization by three regimens according to the frequency and doses of the recombinant vaccine used.
TL;DR / study gist: A randomized trial in 990 Pakistani children showed that a reduced 2-dose schedule (10 µg) and a fractional 3-dose schedule (5 µg) of a recombinant hepatitis B vaccine both provided >99% seroprotection, matching the standard 3-dose (10 µg) regimen.
Original abstract: A controlled, randomized trial was conducted in urban areas of Karachi and Lahore with the aim to look for ways to improve the cost-effectiveness of hepatitis B vaccination. Children under 15 years old (including neonates) were selected and screened for immunization by three regimens according to the frequency and doses of the recombinant vaccine used (Heberbiovac HB, Heber Biotec, Havana). Group A received 10 µg at 0, 1 months; group B (control) received 10 µg at 0, 1 and 2 months (standard regime), and group C received 5 µg at 0, 1 and 2 months. Antibody levels were titrated 2 months after the last dose. Cut-off for seroprotection and hyperresponse were taken as 10 and 100 IU/L, respectively. Nine hundred and ninety children were included and evaluated after discarding those positive for serological hepatitis virus infection markers. Seroprotection rates were 100, 99.7 and 99.7%, and hyperresponse was achieved by 92.7, 99.4, and 97% of the vaccinees in groups A, B, and C, respectively. The same good result was obtained in extreme ages subgroups (≤1 year and ≥10 years old). The 1-year follow up of the children from Karachi showed good persistence of seroprotection (98, 100, and 99.4%) and hyperresponse (79.7, 96.7, and 87.4%). It is concluded that it is feasible to improve the cost-benefit ratio and compliance of hepatitis B vaccination by means of a two-shots or reduced dose schedule of the vaccine employed in the trial.

Title: Phase 1/2 trial of SARS-CoV-2 vaccine ChAdOx1 nCoV-19 with a booster dose induces multifunctional antibody responses
Authors: Jordan R. Barrett, Sandra Belij-Rammerstorfer, Christina Dold, Katie J. Ewer, Pedro M. Folegatti, Ciaran Gilbride, et al.
Study design: Phase 1/2, single-blinded randomized controlled trial (subgroup analysis)
Comparator (full dose / standard / other: SD/SD D56 (standard dose prime and boost 56 days apart), MenACWY comparator, and SD/SD D28 (historical data)
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): SD/LD D56 (standard dose prime and low/half-dose boost 56 days apart)
Schedule / timing: Prime vaccination followed by a booster dose 56 days later (or 28 days for the earlier sub-cohort)
Sample size analyzed: 72 (20 SD/SD D56, 32 SD/LD D56, 10 MenACWY, 10 SD/SD D28)
Primary endpoint: Safety and exploratory humoral and cellular immunogenicity
Immunogenicity endpoint: Anti-spike IgG, NAbs (live and pseudovirus), antibody-dependent phagocytosis (ADNP/ADMP), complement deposition (ADCD), NK cell activation (ADNKA), and T cell responses (IFN-y ELISpot)
Safety endpoint: Solicited local and systemic adverse events (e.g., pyrexia, tenderness)
NI result: N/A
Key numerical result(s): Median normalized NAb titers 14d post-boost: 395 (SD/SD D56), 170 (SD/LD D56), and 274 (SD/SD D28). Reactogenicity dropped substantially on the 2nd dose (0% pyrexia vs 28.8% at prime).
Reason not meta-analyzable: N/A
Data extraction notes: Subgroup analysis focusing on the addition of a booster dose at either standard or low dose spacing, demonstrating that reduced dose spacing (half-dose boost) is immunogenic but yielded lower NAb titers and similar functionally enhanced antibodies compared to standard dose boost.
Screening note: Important sub-study analyzing homologous boosting (full vs half dose) for the ChAdOx1 nCoV-19 vaccine.
Abstract: We describe safety and exploratory humoral and cellular immunogenicity of the vaccine, from subgroups of volunteers in that trial, who were subsequently allocated to receive a homologous full-dose (SD/SD D56; n = 20) or half-dose (SD/LD D56; n = 32) ChAdOx1 booster vaccine 56 d following prime vaccination.
TL;DR / study gist: A Phase 1/2 trial subgroup analysis showed that a homologous booster dose (full or half-dose) of the ChAdOx1 nCoV-19 vaccine at 56 days was safe, less reactogenic than the prime dose, and significantly enhanced both neutralizing and functional anti-spike antibody responses.
Original abstract: More than 190 vaccines are currently in development to prevent infection by the novel severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2. Animal studies suggest that while neutralizing antibodies against the viral spike protein may correlate with protection, additional antibody functions may also be important in preventing infection. Previously, we reported early immunogenicity and safety outcomes of a viral vector coronavirus vaccine, ChAdOx1 nCoV-19 (AZD1222), in a single-blinded phase 1/2 randomized controlled trial of healthy adults aged 18-55 years (NCT04324606). Now we describe safety and exploratory humoral and cellular immunogenicity of the vaccine, from subgroups of volunteers in that trial, who were subsequently allocated to receive a homologous full-dose (SD/SD D56; n = 20) or half-dose (SD/LD D56; n = 32) ChAdOx1 booster vaccine 56 d following prime vaccination. Previously reported immunogenicity data from the open-label 28-d interval prime-boost group (SD/SD D28; n = 10) are also presented to facilitate comparison. Additionally, we describe volunteers boosted with the comparator vaccine (MenACWY; n = 10). In this interim report, we demonstrate that a booster dose of ChAdOx1 nCoV-19 is safe and better tolerated than priming doses. Using a systems serology approach we also demonstrate that anti-spike neutralizing antibody titers, as well as Fc-mediated functional antibody responses, including antibody-dependent neutrophil/monocyte phagocytosis, complement activation and natural killer cell activation, are substantially enhanced by a booster dose of vaccine. A booster dose of vaccine induced stronger antibody responses than a dose-sparing half-dose boost, although the magnitude of T cell responses did not increase with either boost dose. These data support the two-dose vaccine regime that is now being evaluated in phase 3 clinical trials.

Title: Safety and immunogenicity of Ad26.COV2.S in adults: A randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled Phase 2a dose-finding study
Authors: Vicky Cárdenas, Mathieu Le Gars, Carla Truyers, Javier Ruiz-Guiñazú, Frank Struyf, Alicia Colfer, Marc Bonten, Alberto Borobia, Emil C. Reisinger, Ingrid M.C. Kamerling, Macaya Douoguih, Jerald Sadoff
Special population: Healthy or medically stable individuals who had not received a prior COVID-19 vaccine
Schedule / timing: Single and 2-dose schedules, intervals of 56 and 84 days; anamnestic response challenge at 4 or 6 months post-primary vaccination
Primary endpoint: Humoral immune responses 28 days after two doses (56-day interval); 28 days after a single dose; 28 days after two doses given 28 or 84-days apart; safety and reactogenicity
Immunogenicity endpoint: S-ELISA, Virus Neutralising Antibodies (VNA), T cell responses (CD4+, CD8+)
Safety endpoint: Solicited adverse events within 7 days, unsolicited AEs within 28 days, SAEs, AEs leading to discontinuation, adverse events of special interest (TTS)
NI result: N/A
Key numerical result(s): At Day 29, VNA GMT across dose levels was 148 (1.25 x 10^10 vp), 170 (2.5 x 10^10 vp), 243 and 253 (5 x 10^10 vp), and 263 (1 x 10^11 vp). At 14 days post-dose 2, VNA GMT was 545 (56-day interval) vs 914 (84-day interval).
Reason not meta-analyzable: N/A
Data extraction notes: Documented dose-response relationship in VNA and S-bAbs. An 84-day interval between doses yielded higher GMTs than a 56-day interval.
Screening note: Phase 2a dose-finding study evaluating fractional doses (1.25e10 and 2.5e10 vp) compared to standard and higher doses.
Abstract: Background: A single dose of Ad26.COV2.S is well-tolerated and effective in preventing moderate-to-severe disease outcomes due to COVID-19. We evaluated the impact of dose level, number of doses, and dose interval on immunogenicity, reactogenicity, and safety...
TL;DR / study gist: Phase 2a study demonstrating a dose-response relationship for Ad26.COV2.S, persistent immune responses up to 6 months, and enhanced immunogenicity with an 84-day versus 56-day prime-boost interval.
Original abstract: Background: A single dose of Ad26.COV2.S is well-tolerated and effective in preventing moderate-to-severe disease outcomes due to COVID-19. We evaluated the impact of dose level, number of doses, and dose interval on immunogenicity, reactogenicity, and safety of Ad26.COV2.S in adults. Anamnestic responses were also explored.

Title: Acute exercise enhancement of pneumococcal vaccination response: A randomised controlled trial of weaker and stronger immune response
Authors: Kate M. Edwards, Meredith A. Pung, Lianne M. Tomfohr, Michael G. Ziegler, John P. Campbell, Mark T. Drayson, Paul J. Mills
Comparator (full dose / standard / other: Full dose (0.5 ml) PPV + resting control; Half dose (0.25 ml) PPV + resting control
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): Half dose (0.25 ml) PPV + 15 min exercise; Full dose (0.5 ml) PPV + 15 min exercise
Schedule / timing: Single dose; Exercise or rest immediately prior to vaccination; 28-day follow-up for antibody assessment
Primary endpoint: Averaged antibody levels to 11 Pn strains at 28 days post-vaccination
Immunogenicity endpoint: Serum Pn IgG levels to 11 specific serotypes at baseline and 28 days
Safety endpoint: Local muscle soreness/pain and limb circumference (surrogate for inflammation)
NI result: N/A
Key numerical result(s): In the half-dose groups, the exercise group showed a significantly greater averaged antibody response than the control group (F(1,54)=7.54, p=.008). In the full-dose groups, there was no significant main effect for group (p=.189).
Reason not meta-analyzable: N/A
Data extraction notes: Evaluated an acute exercise behavioural adjuvant with standard and fractional dose PPV. The fractional dose + rest yielded a weaker response, but the fractional dose + exercise boosted the response significantly.
Screening note: Evaluates fractional dosing (half dose) of PPV in conjunction with an exercise intervention in young healthy adults.
Abstract: Acute exercise at the time of vaccination can enhance subsequent immune responses. However, the potential benefit of this effect will be its efficacy in boosting poor responses, and thus protection in at-risk populations. The current study tested the effect of exercise on the response to either a full- or half-dose Pneumococcal (Pn) vaccination...
TL;DR / study gist: A 15-minute acute exercise session immediately prior to vaccination significantly enhanced the antibody response to a half-dose pneumococcal vaccine, but did not significantly enhance the already strong response to a full-dose vaccine.
Original abstract: Acute exercise at the time of vaccination can enhance subsequent immune responses. However, the potential benefit of this effect will be its efficacy in boosting poor responses, and thus protection in at-risk populations. The current study tested the effect of exercise on the response to either a full- or half-dose Pneumococcal (Pn) vaccination to elicit stronger and weaker responses. Subjects were 133 young healthy adults, randomised to one of four groups: exercise or control task, receiving a full- or half-dose Pn vaccination. Prior to vaccination, exercise groups completed a 15 min arm and shoulder exercise task, control groups rested quietly. Antibody levels to 11 Pn strains were evaluated at baseline and 1-month. Across all participants, exercise groups showed significantly greater increase in antibody levels than control groups. When doses were compared, it emerged that those who exercised had significantly larger responses than those who rested in the half-dose group, but in the full-dose groups responses were similar. This data indicates the effectiveness of exercise as a vaccine adjuvant, particularly in weaker responses. Thus, given the potential public health benefits of no-cost behavioural intervention to enhance response to vaccination, testing in at-risk populations should be pursued.

Title: A phase II randomized controlled trial of a multicomponent meningococcal serogroup B vaccine, 4CMenB, in infants (II)
Authors: Susanna Esposito, Roman Prymula, Gian Vincenzo Zuccotti, Fang Xie, Michelangelo Barone, Peter M Dull & Daniela Toneatto
Vaccine / intervention: 4CMenB with varying doses of OMV and recombinant proteins
Country / setting: Czech Republic, Italy, Hungary, Chile, Argentina
Study design: Phase II randomized controlled multicenter trial
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): rMenB (0 OMV), rMenB + 1/4 OMV, rMenB + 1/2 OMV, 1/2 dose 4CMenB
Schedule / timing: 2, 3, 4, and 12 months of age
Sample size analyzed: 1137 for safety; 169-173 per group for immunogenicity per-protocol at 5 months
Primary endpoint: Safety/reactogenicity within 3 days of dose 1, and immune responses 1 month post-dose 3
Immunogenicity endpoint: hSBA against fHbp, NadA, and NZ OMV indicator strains
Safety endpoint: Solicited reactions within 7 days, AEs, and SAEs
NI result: N/A
Key numerical result(s): Post-primary hSBA $\ge5$ to NZ OMV: 56% (1/4 OMV), 67% (1/2 OMV), 78% (full OMV). Fever $\ge38.5^\circ$C after dose 1: 13% for rMenB (no OMV) vs 33-53% for OMV-containing groups.
Reason not meta-analyzable: N/A
Data extraction notes: Reducing/removing OMV decreased reactogenicity but unacceptably lowered immunogenicity. Half-dose 4CMenB had lower GMTs for fHbp and NadA compared to full dose.
Screening note: Extracted arm data for multiple fractional OMV/protein dose groups
Abstract: We investigated whether reducing the OMV and/or protein content influences 4CMenB immunogenicity and reactogenicity in healthy two month-old infants... Decreasing or removing the OMV content reduced reactogenicity... but had an unacceptable negative impact on the immunogenicity profile.
TL;DR / study gist: Reducing the OMV dose in 4CMenB lowers infant fever incidence but unacceptably diminishes the vaccine's protective immune response.
Original abstract: We investigated whether reducing the OMV and/or protein content influences 4CMenB immunogenicity and reactogenicity in healthy two month-old infants... Decreasing or removing the OMV content reduced reactogenicity... but had an unacceptable negative impact on the immunogenicity profile.

Title: Comparison of the immunogenicity and safety of a split-virion, inactivated, trivalent influenza vaccine (Fluzone®) administered by intradermal and intramuscular route in healthy adults
Authors: Robert W. Frenck Jr., Robert Belshe, Rebecca C. Brady, Patricia L. Winokur, James D. Campbell, John Treanor, Christine M. Hay, Cornelia L. Dekker, Emmanuel B. Walter Jr., Thomas R. Cate, Kathryn M. Edwards, Heather Hill, Mark Wolff, Tom LeDuc, Nadia Tornieporth
Vaccine / intervention: Split-virion, inactivated, trivalent influenza vaccine (TIV, Fluzone)
Study design: Multi-center, randomized partially blinded phase II clinical trial
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): 9 µg ID (BD Soluvia), 6 µg ID (BD Soluvia), 3 µg ID (Mantoux)
Schedule / timing: Single dose, follow-up blood draw at day 21
Primary endpoint: Anti-HA antibody geometric mean titers (GMTs) for each antigen at 21 days post-vaccination
Immunogenicity endpoint: GMTs, seroprotection (HAI $\ge$ 1:40), seroconversion (4-fold rise)
Safety endpoint: Solicited local/systemic AEs (7 days), unsolicited AEs (21 days), SAEs (6 months), VAS pain score
Non-inferiority margin: Upper limit of the two-sided 95% CI for the difference in log10 GMTs (IM minus ID) < log10(1.5) (approx 0.176)
NI result: Margin met for 6 µg and 9 µg ID vs 15 µg IM for all 3 strains overall; margin NOT met for 3 µg ID (except H3N2) and NOT met for 6 µg ID in older cohort (50-64y) for H1N1 and B.
Key numerical result(s): Post-vaccination GMTs (18-64y overall): A/H3N2 [IM=593; 9µg ID=615; 6µg ID=566; 3µg ID=469]. A/H1N1 [IM=121; 9µg ID=116; 6µg ID=105; 3µg ID=89]. B [IM=87; 9µg ID=83; 6µg ID=79; 3µg ID=55]. Erythema occurred in ~73-74% of ID groups vs 3% in IM group.
Reason not meta-analyzable: N/A
Data extraction notes: Clear demonstration of dose-sparing potential for 18-49y using 6µg or 9µg ID via microinjection, but 6µg fell short in the 50-64y cohort. ID administration caused much higher rates of local reactogenicity (erythema, swelling) compared to IM but was highly acceptable to participants.
Screening note: Compares multiple fractional ID doses (using BD Soluvia vs Mantoux) against standard IM administration for influenza.
Abstract: The aim of the study was to determine whether reduced doses of trivalent inactivated influenza vaccine (TIV) administered by the intradermal (ID) route generated similar immune responses to standard TIV given intramuscularly (IM) with comparable safety profiles... In conclusion, for the aggregated cohorts of adults 18-64 years of age, reduced doses (6 µg and 9 µg) of TIV delivered ID using a novel microinjection system stimulated comparable HAI antibody responses to standard TIV given IM.
TL;DR / study gist: Reduced intradermal doses (6 µg and 9 µg) of influenza vaccine yield similar immune responses to the standard 15 µg intramuscular dose in adults, although responses are poorer with 3 µg ID or in older adults (50-64 years) given 6 µg ID.
Original abstract: The aim of the study was to determine whether reduced doses of trivalent inactivated influenza vaccine (TIV) administered by the intradermal (ID) route generated similar immune responses to standard TIV given intramuscularly (IM) with comparable safety profiles. Recent changes in immunization recommendations have increased the number of people for whom influenza vaccination is recommended. Thus, given this increased need and intermittent vaccine shortages, means to rapidly expand the vaccine supply are needed. Previously healthy subjects 18-64 years of age were randomly assigned to one of four TIV vaccine groups: standard 15 µg HA/strain TIV IM, either 9 µg or 6 µg HA/strain of TIV ID given using a new microinjection system (BD Soluvia™ Microinjection System), or 3 µg HA/strain of TIV ID given by Mantoux technique. All vaccines contained A/New Caledonia (H1N1), A/Wyoming (H3N2) and B/Jiangsu strains of influenza. Sera were obtained 21 days after vaccination and hemagglutination inhibition (HAI) assays were performed and geometric mean titers (GMT) were compared among the groups. Participants were queried immediately following vaccination regarding injection pain and quality of the experience. Local and systemic reactions were collected for 7 days following vaccination and compared. Ten study sites enrolled 1592 subjects stratified by age; 18-49 years (N = 814) and 50-64 years (N = 778). Among all subjects, for each of the three vaccine strains, the GMTs at 21 days post-vaccination for both the 9 µg and the 6 µg doses of each strain given ID were non inferior to GMTs generated after standard 15 µg doses/strain IM. However, for the 3 µg ID dose, only the A/Wyoming antigen produced a GMT that was non-inferior to the standard IM dose. Additionally, in the subgroup of subjects 50-64 years of age, the 6 µg dose given ID induced GMTs that were inferior to the standard IM TIV for the A/H1N1 and B strains. No ID dose produced a GMT superior to that seen after standard IM TIV. Local erythema and swelling were significantly more common in the ID groups but the reactions were mild to moderate and short-lived. No significant safety issues related to intradermal administration were identified. Participants given TIV ID provided favorable responses to questions about their experiences with ID administration. In conclusion, for the aggregated cohorts of adults 18-64 years of age, reduced doses (6 µg and 9 µg) of TIV delivered ID using a novel microinjection system stimulated comparable HAI antibody responses to standard TIV given IM. The reduced 3 µg dose administered ID by needle and syringe, as well as the 6 µg ID for subjects aged 50-64 years of age generated poorer immune responses as compared to the 15 µg IM dose.

Title: MAS-1, a novel water-in-oil adjuvant/delivery system, with reduced seasonal influenza vaccine hemagglutinin dose may enhance potency, durability and cross-reactivity of antibody responses in the elderly
Authors: Geoffrey J. Gorse, Stephen Grimes, Helen Buck, Hussain Mulla, Peter White, Heather Hill, Jeanine May, Sharon E. Frey, Peter Blackburn
Vaccine / intervention: MAS-1-adjuvanted reduced dose inactivated seasonal influenza virus vaccine (IIV)
Study design: Phase 1, randomized, double-blind, safety and immunogenicity, adjuvant dose escalation trial
Comparator (full dose / standard / other: High dose (HD) IIV (Fluzone HD containing 60 $\mu$g per HA)
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): 9 $\mu$g per HA in 0.3 mL MAS-1; 9 $\mu$g per HA in 0.5 mL MAS-1
Schedule / timing: Single dose on Day 0 with follow-ups up to Day 366 (Day 85 booster optional)
Primary endpoint: HAI antibody responses (GMT, seroconversion rate, GMFI) against vaccine strains
Immunogenicity endpoint: HAI antibody responses to vaccine and cross-reactive historical strains (GMT, GMFI, seroconversion, seroprotection)
Safety endpoint: Reactogenicity, adverse events, SAEs, AESIs, and safety laboratory measures
NI result: N/A
Key numerical result(s): The 0.3 mL MAS-1 dose was safe, but 0.5 mL MAS-1 caused late ISRs in 4/10 subjects. At Days 85 and 169, GMFIs to A (H3N2) were higher for the MAS-1 group than HD IIV without overlapping 95% CIs.
Reason not meta-analyzable: N/A
Data extraction notes: Phase 1B evaluated 0.3 mL MAS-1 (n=15) vs HD IIV (n=15); Phase 1B Extension evaluated 0.5 mL MAS-1 (n=10) vs HD IIV (n=5).
Screening note: Extracted standard data for fractional/reduced dose IIV influenza vaccine vs HD IIV.
Abstract: Background: Increased influenza vaccine efficacy is needed in the elderly at high-risk for morbidity and mortality due to influenza infection. Adjuvants may allow hemagglutinin (HA) dose-sparing with enhanced immunogenicity... Conclusion: MAS-1 adjuvant provided HA dose-sparing without safety concerns at the 0.3 mL dose...
TL;DR / study gist: A 9 $\mu$g fractional HA dose of IIV formulated in 0.3 mL of MAS-1 adjuvant was safe and provided more durable, cross-reactive HAI antibody responses over 6 months in the elderly compared to a 60 $\mu$g high-dose unadjuvanted vaccine.
Original abstract: Background: Increased influenza vaccine efficacy is needed in the elderly at high-risk for morbidity and mortality due to influenza infection. Adjuvants may allow hemagglutinin (HA) dose-sparing with enhanced immunogenicity. MAS-1 is an investigational water-in-oil emulsion-based adjuvant/delivery system comprised of stable nanoglobular aqueous droplets. Methods: A phase 1, randomized, double-blind, safety and immunogenicity, adjuvant dose escalation trial was conducted in persons aged 65 years and older. MAS-1 adjuvant dose volumes at 0.3 mL or 0.5 mL containing 9 $\mu$g per HA derived from licensed seasonal trivalent influenza vaccine (IIV, Fluzone HD 60 $\mu$g per HA, Sanofi Pasteur) were compared to high dose (HD) IIV (Fluzone HD). Safety was measured by reactogenicity, adverse events, and safety laboratory measures. Immunogenicity was assessed by serum hemagglutination inhibition (HAI) antibody titers. Results: Forty-five subjects, aged 65-83 years, were randomly assigned to receive 9 $\mu$g per HA in 0.3 mL MAS-1 (15 subjects) or HD IIV (15 subjects) followed by groups randomly assigned to receive 9 $\mu$g per HA in 0.5 mL MAS-1 (10 subjects) or HD IIV (5 subjects). Injection site tenderness, induration, and pain, and headache, myalgia, malaise and fatigue were common, resolving before day 14 post-vaccination. Clinically significant late-onset injection site reactions occurred in four of ten subjects at the 0.5 mL adjuvant dose. Safety laboratory measures were within acceptable limits. MAS-1-adjuvanted IIV enhanced mean antibody titers, mean-fold increases in antibody titer, and seroconversion rates against vaccine strains for at least 168 days post-vaccination and enhanced cross-reactive antibodies against some non-study vaccine influenza viruses. Conclusion: MAS-1 adjuvant provided HA dose-sparing without safety concerns at the 0.3 mL dose, but the 0.5 mL dose caused late injection site reactions. MAS-1-adjuvanted IIV induced higher HAI antibody responses with prolonged durability including against historical strains, thereby providing greater potential vaccine efficacy in the elderly throughout an influenza season.

Title: Comparison of Intramuscular and Intradermal Applications of Hepatitis B Vaccine in Hemodialysis Patients
Authors: M. Kasım Karahocagil, Turan Buzgan, Hasan Irmak, Hasan Karsen, Hayrettin Akdeniz, Nevzat Akman
Country / setting: Turkey (Hemodialysis Unit of Yüzüncü Yıl University)
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): 2 $\mu$g reduced dose (one-tenth) ID
Schedule / timing: IM: 0, 1, 2, and 6 months (booster at 12m if needed). ID: 6 monthly doses (0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 months) + additional doses up to 12 if needed.
Primary endpoint: Anti-HBs antibody responses
Immunogenicity endpoint: Seroprotection (Anti-HBs $\ge$ 10 mIU/mL), Seroconversion (Anti-HBs $\ge$ 1 mIU/mL), and GMT
Safety endpoint: General symptoms (fever, fatigue, malaise) and local symptoms (pain, discoloration)
NI result: N/A
Key numerical result(s): At month 7, seroprotection (Sp) was 83.3% for IM vs 80.8% for ID. At month 13, Sp was 92.6% for IM vs 95.8% for ID. There was no statistically significant difference between the Sp rates or GMTs in both groups at months 7 and 13 (p>0.05).
Reason not meta-analyzable: N/A
Data extraction notes: ID group received 1/10th the dose but had a more accelerated/frequent dosing schedule (monthly for 6 months).
Screening note: Extracted standard data comparing fractional dose ID to standard dose IM hepatitis B vaccination in hemodialysis patients.
Abstract: This study compared the application of intramuscular recombinant hepatitis B vaccine in hemodialysis patients with the application of accelerated intradermal recombinant hepatitis B vaccine... As a result, in the vaccination of hemodialysis patients against hepatitis B, the accelerated ID application of hepatitis B vaccine with a dose reduced to one-tenth is more cost-effective than the standard dose vaccination schedules.
TL;DR / study gist: An accelerated intradermal regimen of hepatitis B vaccine at a one-tenth fractional dose (2 $\mu$g) provides comparable seroprotection rates to the standard intramuscular regimen (20 $\mu$g) in hemodialysis patients, significantly reducing costs.
Original abstract: This study compared the application of intramuscular recombinant hepatitis B vaccine in hemodialysis patients with the application of accelerated intradermal recombinant hepatitis B vaccine, which can be applied with one-tenth of the standard dose. Sixty seronegative patients for hepatitis B were randomly separated into two groups. Twenty $\mu$g of the recombinant hepatitis B vaccine was intramuscularly applied at 0-, 1-, 2-, and 6-month intervals to the first group (32 cases). One more dose was applied at month 12 to those whose anti-HBs titers remained below 100 mIU/mL at month 7. The same vaccine was intradermally applied at 2 $\mu$g dose six times with one-month intervals to the second group (28 cases). Vaccine applications were continued in those whose anti-HBs titers remained below 100 mIU/mL at month 7 until antibody titers reached above this value or until the dose number became 12. Measurements of antibody titers were repeated at month 13 in both groups. As a result, in the vaccination of hemodialysis patients against hepatitis B, the accelerated ID application of hepatitis B vaccine with a dose reduced to one-tenth is more cost-effective than the standard dose vaccination schedules. Especially for hemodialysis patients, the time has come for routine application of ID hepatitis B vaccine as an alternative vaccination method.

Title: Safety and immunogenicity of CPG 7909 injection as an adjuvant to Fluarix influenza vaccine
Authors: C.L. Cooper, H.L. Davis, M.L. Morris, S.M. Efler, A.M. Krieg, Y. Li, C. Laframboise, M.J. Al Adhami, Y. Khaliq, I. Seguin, D.W. Cameron
Vaccine / intervention: Trivalent killed split influenza vaccine (Fluarix) with or without CPG 7909 adjuvant
Special population: Healthy individuals with low pre-existing immunity ($\le$ 20 HI) to specific strains
Study design: Phase Ib, randomized, controlled, double-blind vaccine study
Comparator (full dose / standard / other: Full-dose Fluarix (15 $\mu$g HA) with saline, Full-dose Fluarix with CPG 7909
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): 1/10th dose Fluarix (1.5 $\mu$g HA) with saline, 1/10th dose Fluarix with CPG 7909
Schedule / timing: Single administration at Day 0 with follow-ups at days 2, 14, 28, and 56.
Primary endpoint: Safety, tolerability, and HI antibody responses
Immunogenicity endpoint: HI activity (titres, seroprotection), anti-HA ELISA (total and IgG isotypes), PBMC antigen-specific IFN-$\gamma$ secretion
Safety endpoint: Adverse events (local and systemic), physical evaluation, laboratory blood assays, assays for DNA autoimmunity
NI result: N/A
Key numerical result(s): The 1/10th dose Fluarix produced similarly high proportions of seroprotective antibody titers as the full-dose vaccine. Addition of CPG 7909 to 1/10th dose Fluarix resulted in significantly higher levels of IFN-$\gamma$ secretion at 4 weeks restimulated with A/Beijing/262/95 ($P=0.048$) and B/Harbin/7/94 ($P=0.0057$) compared to 1/10th dose control.
Reason not meta-analyzable: N/A
Data extraction notes: Sequential enrollment. Cohort 1 evaluated 1/10th dose $\pm$ CPG 7909 (n=30), Cohort 2 evaluated full dose $\pm$ CPG 7909 (n=30). The 1/10th dose alone was surprisingly highly immunogenic for HI titres.
Screening note: Good paper comparing 1/10th dose of influenza vaccine to full dose, investigating an adjuvant's ability to spare dose and restore cellular immune responses.
Abstract: CPG 7909, a 24-mer B-Class CpG oligodeoxynucleotide (ODN), was tested for safety, tolerability and its ability to augment the immunogenicity of a commercial trivalent killed split influenza vaccine... These results suggest that addition of CPG 7909 to Fluarix may allow the use of reduced vaccine doses without reduced immunogenicity.
TL;DR / study gist: A 1/10th fractional dose of Fluarix influenza vaccine produced similar seroprotective HI antibody titers to a full dose in healthy adults, and adding CPG 7909 adjuvant restored cellular immune responses (IFN-$\gamma$) to full-dose levels.
Original abstract: CPG 7909, a 24-mer B-Class CpG oligodeoxynucleotide (ODN), was tested for safety, tolerability and its ability to augment the immunogenicity of a commercial trivalent killed split influenza vaccine (Fluarix® containing A/Beijing/262/95, A/Sydney/5/97 and B/Harbin/7/94; SmithKline Beecham) in a phase Ib blinded, randomized, controlled clinical trial. Sixty healthy volunteers were recruited in two consecutive cohorts of 30 subjects, who were randomly assigned to receive Fluarix plus 1 mg CPG 7909 or Fluarix plus saline control (15 subjects each). Vaccines were administered by intramuscular injection on a single occasion with subjects in the first cohort receiving a 1/10th dose of Fluarix and those in the second cohort receiving the full-dose. All safety measures including physical evaluation, laboratory blood assays, and assays for DNA autoimmunity were within normal values except for transient and clinically inconsequential decreases in total white blood cell counts in groups receiving CPG 7909. All vaccines were found to be generally well tolerated with similar frequency and intensity for most adverse reactions for groups receiving CPG 7909 as controls. Exceptions were injection site pain and headache, which were reduced in frequency in subjects receiving the 1/10th Fluarix dose without CpG, compared to the frequency in all other groups. There was a lack of pre-existing immunity, defined as hemagglutinin inhibition (HI) activity $\le$20 for all subjects to the influenza strains A/Beijing/262/95 and B/Harbin/7/94 and for some subjects to A/Sydney/5/97. Post-vaccination humoral immune responses, as determined 2 and 4 weeks later by assay of HI activity and ELISA to detect antibodies against hemagglutinin (anti-HA) were similar for both full and reduced Fluarix doses but the cellular immune responses (measured as PBMC antigen-specific IFN-$\gamma$ secretion) were reduced in the 1/10th Fluarix dose group. Humoral responses were not significantly enhanced by the addition of CPG 7909, except in individuals with pre-existing immunity to A/Sydney/5/97 strain (baseline HI activity titre >20), where there was a trend to higher HI activity with CPG 7909 ($P=0.06$). The addition of CPG 7909 to the 1/10th dose of Fluarix did however result in significantly higher levels of IFN-$\gamma$ secretion from peripheral blood mononuclear cells recovered at 4 weeks and restimulated ex vivo with A/Beijing/262/95 ($P=0.048$) and B/Harbin/7/94 ($P=0.0057$) restoring these to the level seen with full-dose vaccine. These results suggest that addition of CPG 7909 to Fluarix may allow the use of reduced vaccine doses without reduced immunogenicity.

Title: Haemophilus influenzae type b conjugate vaccine diluted tenfold in diphtheria-tetanus-whole cell pertussis vaccine: a randomized trial
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): 1/10 dose (0.05 ml equivalent reconstituted in DTP)
Schedule / timing: 3 doses (6, 10, 14 weeks) + booster at 9 months
Primary endpoint: Seroprotection (anti-PRP ≥1.0 μg/ml)
Immunogenicity endpoint: Anti-PRP IgG concentration (≥1.0 μg/ml) and booster response
Safety endpoint: Safety (implied as safe)
NI result: N/A
Key numerical result(s): Anti-PRP ≥1.0 μg/ml (post-primary): Full dose = 95%; 1/10 dose = 94%. Booster Response (GMT): Full dose = 68.55 μg/ml; 1/10 dose = 151.36 μg/ml (P=0.009).
Reason not meta-analyzable: N/A
Data extraction notes: The 1/10 dose was reconstituted in a 10-dose DTP vial. Surprisingly, the fractional dose showed a significantly higher booster response than the full dose.
Screening note: Dose-sparing via dilution in DTP multidose vials
TL;DR / study gist: A 1/10 fractional dose of Hib vaccine diluted in DTP was as immunogenic as the full dose and showed superior priming for immunological memory.

Title: Immune response of infants to fractional doses of intradermally administered inactivated poliovirus vaccine
Comparator (full dose / standard / other: Standard IM doses are cited for comparison
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): 0.1 mL intradermal IPV (1/5th of 0.5ml dose)
Schedule / timing: Group A: 2 doses (8 weeks apart); Group B: 3 doses (4 weeks apart)
Primary endpoint: Seroresponse to poliovirus types 1,2, and 3
Immunogenicity endpoint: Seroconversion rates (4 weeks post-last dose) and GMT
Safety endpoint: Not reported in abstract
NI result: N/A
Key numerical result(s): Seroconversion rates (Group A vs Group B): Type 1: 90% vs 90%; Type 2: 70% vs 80%; Type 3: 97% vs 98%. Seroconversion index: 85.5 (Group A) vs 89.0 (Group B).
Reason not meta-analyzable: N/A
Data extraction notes: Excluded 9 infants (1 received OPV, 8 protocol deviations). Baseline maternal antibody was present in 87% (Type 1), 86% (Type 2), and 71% (Type 3).
Screening note: Randomized trial comparing 2 vs 3 fractional ID doses.
TL;DR / study gist: Fractional ID doses of IPV (1/5th dose) are highly immunogenic in infants, providing a potential cost-saving strategy for polio eradication.

Title: Safety, tolerability, and immunogenicity of an inactivated SARS-CoV-2 vaccine (CoronaVac) in healthy adults aged 60 years and older: a randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled, phase 1/2 clinical trial
Population age group: Healthy adults aged 60 years and older
Fractional / reduced dose arm(s): 1.5 µg, 3 µg, 6 µg
Schedule / timing: Two doses on days 0 and 28
Primary endpoint: Safety (adverse reactions within 28 days post-injection) and Immunogenicity (seroconversion at day 28 post-second dose)
Immunogenicity endpoint: Seroconversion rate and GMT of neutralizing antibodies to live SARS-CoV-2
Safety endpoint: Seroconversion rate and GMT of neutralizing antibodies to live SARS-CoV-2
